Condition 2: Majority of TOTAL membership = more than half of 250 = at least 126 (50% of 250 = 125, so more than 125 = 126 minimum). The amendment bill requires BOTH conditions to be satisfied simultaneously. Since 120 (2/3 of present+voting) is LESS than 126 (majority of total), the binding constraint is Condition 2. The minimum 'YES' votes needed = 126 (which simultaneously satisfies both: 126 > 120, so it also exceeds 2/3 of present+voting threshold). Answer: 126 votes minimum. Option B is correct.

questions.question: In India, the concept of 'Judicial Activism' has led to expansion of rights through Public Interest Litigation (PIL). PIL was introduced in the Indian legal system primarily through the efforts of which Supreme Court judge?
options: Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and Justice P.N. Bhagwati
Justice H.R. Khanna
Justice Y.V. Chandrachud
Justice M.N. Venkatachaliah
questions.explanation: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in India was pioneered primarily by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and Justice P.N. Bhagwati in the late 1970s and early 1980s. They liberalized the concept of locus standi — traditionally, only an aggrieved person could file a petition. Through PIL, any public-spirited citizen could approach the court on behalf of those who could not access justice (the poor, marginalized, etc.). Justice Bhagwati's judgment in S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981) is a landmark in establishing PIL as a tool of judicial activism. Justice Krishna Iyer's innovations in interpreting Article 21 expansively also contributed significantly. Together, they transformed Indian constitutional jurisprudence.

questions.question:
Under Article 109 (Special procedure regarding Money Bills), consider the following timeline:
- Day 1: Lok Sabha passes a Money Bill and sends it to Rajya Sabha.
- Day 12: Rajya Sabha sends the bill back with recommendations.
- Day 15: Lok Sabha considers but rejects all Rajya Sabha recommendations.
- Day 16: Lok Sabha passes the bill again without Rajya Sabha amendments.

What happens next?
options: The bill goes back to Rajya Sabha for final concurrence since it rejected all recommendations
The bill is deemed to have been passed by both Houses in its original Lok Sabha form and is sent to the President for assent
A joint sitting of Parliament must be called to resolve the disagreement on recommendations
The bill lapses as it has been pending for more than 14 days
questions.explanation: Under Article 109, the procedure for Money Bills is: (1) Money Bill is introduced and passed ONLY in Lok Sabha. (2) It is sent to Rajya Sabha which can make recommendations but cannot amend or reject it. Rajya Sabha must return the bill within 14 days. (3) After Rajya Sabha returns the bill (with or without recommendations), Lok Sabha may accept or reject any/all recommendations. (4) The bill is then deemed to have been PASSED BY BOTH HOUSES — whether Lok Sabha accepts or rejects Rajya Sabha's recommendations. The bill is then presented to the President for assent. Rajya Sabha cannot force a joint sitting over a Money Bill (unlike ordinary bills under Article 108). The timeline shows Rajya Sabha returned it on Day 12 (within 14 days), so there is no lapse. Option B is correct.

questions.question: The Election Commission of India is empowered to recognize political parties and allot symbols. A political party can be recognized as a 'National Party' if it fulfills certain criteria. Which of the following is a CORRECT criterion for national party recognition by ECI?
options: A party must win at least 11 seats in the Lok Sabha in a general election
A party must secure at least 6% of valid votes polled in any 4 or more states in a general election AND win 4 Lok Sabha seats
A party must have governments in at least 5 states simultaneously
A party must have been registered with ECI for at least 10 years
questions.explanation: Under the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968, a party is recognized as a National Party if it fulfills ANY ONE of these conditions: (1) Secures at least 6% of valid votes in 4 or more states in a Lok Sabha or state assembly election AND wins at least 4 seats in the Lok Sabha; OR (2) Wins at least 2% of seats in the Lok Sabha (currently 11 seats in a 543-seat house) from at least 3 different states; OR (3) Is recognized as a State Party in at least 4 states. Option B correctly describes one of these criteria (6% in 4 states + 4 Lok Sabha seats). Option A is partially correct (11 seats) but incomplete without the 3-state criterion. Options C and D are not actual criteria.

questions.question:
Parliament passes the 'XYZ Act' placing it in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution to protect it from judicial challenge. The Act was placed in the Ninth Schedule on January 1, 2005. An aggrieved party challenges the Act before the Supreme Court in 2024.

Can the Supreme Court review this Act?
options: No, Ninth Schedule laws are completely immune from judicial review
Yes, but only on procedural grounds related to the amendment process
Yes, because as per I.R. Coelho judgment (2007), laws placed in the Ninth Schedule after April 24, 1973 can be reviewed if they violate the basic structure
No, once Parliament places a law in the Ninth Schedule, it acquires permanent immunity
questions.explanation: In I.R. Coelho (by LIC) v. State of Tamil Nadu (2007), a 9-judge constitutional bench unanimously held that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule on or after April 24, 1973 (the date of the Kesavananda Bharati judgment) can be challenged if they violate the basic structure of the Constitution. Laws placed in the Ninth Schedule before April 24, 1973 retain their immunity. The rationale is that the basic structure doctrine, established in 1973, cannot be circumvented by Parliament placing laws in the Ninth Schedule. Since the XYZ Act was placed in the Ninth Schedule on January 1, 2005 — which is AFTER April 24, 1973 — it is fully subject to judicial review under the basic structure doctrine. The Supreme Court CAN review it.

questions.question: Right to Education Act (RTE) 2009 was enacted to give effect to Article 21A. What minimum percentage of seats must private unaided non-minority schools reserve for children from economically weaker sections (EWS) in elementary education?
questions.explanation: The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 (RTE Act) mandates under Section 12(1)(c) that every private unaided non-minority school shall admit in Class I, to the extent of at least 25% of the strength of that class, children belonging to weaker sections and disadvantaged groups in the neighbourhood. This 25% reservation applies to private unaided schools (not government schools or minority-run institutions). The government reimburses such schools for these seats at government school rates. This provision was upheld by the Supreme Court in Society for Unaided Private Schools of Rajasthan v. Union of India (2012) — a 3:2 majority judgment.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In India, there is a single integrated judicial system with the Supreme Court at the apex.
Reason (R): Unlike the USA, India does not have a separate federal judicial system and state judicial system — all courts are part of one unified hierarchy.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. India has a single unified/integrated judicial system (unlike the USA where there are separate federal courts and state courts). In India: Supreme Court → High Courts → Subordinate Courts (District Courts, etc.) form one continuous hierarchy. All courts are interconnected through the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction. High Courts have supervisory jurisdiction over all subordinate courts in their territories. Even though High Courts are established under the Constitution and are state courts, they are part of the same integrated system with appeals going to the Supreme Court. This is in stark contrast to the USA where many cases never reach the federal court system. R correctly explains why India has a 'single integrated' judicial system — the absence of a parallel federal court hierarchy.

questions.question:
A citizen files a PIL challenging a Central Government notification on the ground that it violates a Directive Principle of State Policy. The government argues that DPSPs are not enforceable by courts.

What is the MOST ACCURATE legal position?
options: The PIL is maintainable and the court can strike down the notification if it violates any DPSP
The PIL is not maintainable as DPSPs are explicitly non-justiciable under Article 37
While DPSPs are non-justiciable as standalone claims, courts have held that DPSPs can be used to test the reasonableness of restrictions on Fundamental Rights and guide statutory interpretation
DPSPs have been made justiciable by the 42nd Amendment and the PIL is fully maintainable
questions.explanation: Article 37 explicitly states that DPSPs shall not be enforceable by any court. Therefore, a court cannot strike down a law or government action SOLELY because it violates a DPSP. However, this does not render DPSPs completely toothless: (1) In Article 19 cases, the Supreme Court can use DPSPs to determine whether restrictions on fundamental rights are 'reasonable' (Chintamani Rao v. State of M.P., 1950 and many subsequent cases). (2) In case of conflict between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs, Article 31C (added by 42nd Amendment) protects laws implementing Articles 39(b) and 39(c) from challenge under Articles 14 and 19. (3) Courts use DPSPs for statutory interpretation. A PIL SOLELY based on DPSP violation is not maintainable, but courts can consider DPSPs alongside FR analysis. Option C is the most accurate nuanced answer.

questions.question: Article 262 of the Constitution relates to adjudication of disputes regarding inter-state rivers or river valleys. Which tribunal was established under the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956 to resolve the Cauvery water dispute?
options: Bachawat Tribunal, 1969
Eradi Tribunal, 1990
Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal (CWDT), 1990
Godavari River Water Disputes Tribunal, 1969
questions.explanation: The Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal (CWDT) was established in 1990 under the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956, enacted under Article 262 of the Constitution. The dispute involves four states: Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Kerala, and Puducherry. The tribunal gave its final award in 2007 (modified in 2007 by the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal). In 2018, the Supreme Court gave a landmark judgment in the Cauvery case (Tamil Nadu v. Karnataka) modifying the allocations. A key feature of Article 262 is that Parliament may by law exclude the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court in respect of inter-state water disputes referred to such tribunal — making the tribunal's award final (barring Supreme Court's intervention under special circumstances).

questions.question:
The following table shows voter turnout in Indian General Elections:

Election | Year | Voter Turnout (%)
1st      | 1952 | 45.7%
5th      | 1971 | 55.3%
8th      | 1984 | 63.6%
10th     | 1991 | 56.7%
14th     | 2004 | 58.1%
15th     | 2009 | 58.2%
16th     | 2014 | 66.4%
17th     | 2019 | 67.4%

Which of the following conclusions is MOST accurately drawn from this data?
options: Voter turnout has increased steadily every election since 1952, showing growing democratic participation
The 1984 election's high turnout was likely driven by a specific political event (sympathy wave), and the recent high turnouts (2014, 2019) reflect improved voter awareness and ECI initiatives
Low turnout in early elections (1952) proves that universal adult franchise was a mistake at Independence
Voter turnout is determined solely by the candidates' profiles and has no correlation with events
questions.explanation: Option A is incorrect — turnout has not increased STEADILY (1991 shows a dip from 1984's 63.6% to 56.7%). Option B is the most analytically valid: the 1984 election's 63.6% peak was clearly driven by the sympathy wave following Indira Gandhi's assassination — a specific political event. The high turnouts in 2014 (66.4%) and 2019 (67.4%) correlate with several factors: increased voter awareness campaigns by ECI, Systematic Voters' Education and Electoral Participation (SVEEP) program, high political polarization, and increased youth voter registration. Option C is analytically inappropriate and logically unsound. Option D is too absolute and ignores the correlation with political events. The data supports Option B as the most historically and analytically grounded conclusion.

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India is established under Article 148 of the Constitution. Which of the following correctly describes the CAG's position?
options: The CAG is appointed by Parliament and is responsible to the Finance Ministry
The CAG is appointed by the President and can be removed only in the same manner as a judge of the Supreme Court
The CAG reports directly to the Prime Minister and submits audit reports to the Cabinet
The CAG's report is submitted to both Houses of Parliament directly without Presidential transmission
questions.explanation: Under Article 148: (1) There shall be a CAG of India appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal. (2) The CAG shall be removed from office only in the same manner as a Judge of the Supreme Court (i.e., by an address of each House of Parliament supported by majority of total membership and 2/3rd majority of members present and voting, presented to the President). (3) The CAG's reports relating to the accounts of the Union shall be submitted to the President, who shall cause them to be laid before each House of Parliament (not directly to Parliament). (4) The CAG is independent of both the executive (not responsible to Finance Ministry) and Parliament (not appointed by Parliament). Option B is correct.

questions.question:
State P's legislature passes a law imposing a tax on professions, trades, callings and employment under Article 276. The maximum annual tax is set at ₹3,000 per person. The State's law is challenged on the ground that it exceeds the constitutional limit.

What is the CONSTITUTIONAL limit under Article 276 and what is the legal position of the State law?
options: The limit is ₹2,500 per year; the law is invalid to the extent it exceeds ₹2,500
The limit is ₹2,500 per year after the 60th Amendment; the law is VALID as amended by the 60th Amendment which raised the limit to ₹2,500
The limit is ₹2,500 per year; the state law imposing ₹3,000 is unconstitutional and void to the extent of excess
There is no constitutional limit under Article 276; states can impose any amount
questions.explanation: Article 276 deals with taxes on professions, trades, callings and employments. Originally the limit was ₹250 per year. The 60th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1988 raised this limit to ₹2,500 per year (per person). The current constitutional maximum under Article 276(2) is ₹2,500 per annum per person. Any state law imposing a tax on professions exceeding ₹2,500 per year would be unconstitutional and void to the extent of excess. Therefore, State P's law imposing ₹3,000 per year exceeds the constitutional limit of ₹2,500 and is unconstitutional to the extent it exceeds ₹2,500. The law would be read down to ₹2,500 (severability) or struck down entirely depending on interpretation. Option C correctly identifies the ₹2,500 limit and invalid nature of the excess.

questions.question: Under which article of the Indian Constitution does the President of India address both Houses of Parliament assembled together at the commencement of the first session after each general election and at the commencement of the first session of each year?
options: Article 85
Article 86
Article 87
Article 88
questions.explanation: Article 87 of the Indian Constitution mandates the special address by the President. It states: (1) At the commencement of the first session after each general election to the House of the People and at the commencement of the first session of each year, the President shall address both Houses of Parliament assembled together and inform Parliament of the causes of its summons. (2) Provision shall be made by the rules regulating the procedure of either House for the allotment of time for discussion of matters referred to in such address. Article 85 deals with sessions of Parliament. Article 86 gives the President the right to address Parliament and send messages. Article 88 relates to rights of Ministers and AG to address Parliament.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the Ordinance-making power of the President (Article 123):

1. The President can promulgate an Ordinance only when both Houses of Parliament are not in session.
2. An Ordinance has the same force as an Act of Parliament.
3. An Ordinance must be laid before both Houses when Parliament re-assembles and ceases to operate 6 weeks from the reassembly of Parliament.
4. The President can withdraw an Ordinance at any time.
5. A re-promulgated Ordinance (promulgated again without Parliamentary approval) has been held constitutional by the Supreme Court.

Which statements are CORRECTLY stated?
options: 1, 2, 3 and 4 only
1, 2 and 4 only
2, 3 and 4 only
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: Article 123 empowers the President to promulgate ordinances when Parliament (either House) is not in session. Statement 2 — CORRECT: An Ordinance has the same force and effect as an Act of Parliament (Article 123(2)). Statement 3 — CORRECT: An Ordinance must be laid before both Houses when Parliament reassembles and ceases to operate at the expiration of 6 weeks from the date of reassembly of Parliament (or earlier if resolutions disapproving it are passed by both Houses). Statement 4 — CORRECT: The President may withdraw an Ordinance at any time (Article 123(2) proviso). Statement 5 — INCORRECT: In D.C. Wadhwa v. State of Bihar (1987), the Supreme Court held that the practice of re-promulgating Ordinances (without approval) is a fraud on the Constitution and unconstitutional — it subverts the democratic legislative process. Hence 5 is false. Options 1, 2, 3, and 4 are correct.

questions.question: The State Finance Commission (SFC) is a constitutional body established under Article 243I (for Panchayats) and Article 243Y (for Municipalities). How often must the Governor constitute a Finance Commission?
options: Every 2 years
Every 3 years
Every 5 years
Every 10 years
questions.explanation: Under Article 243I (for Panchayats) and Article 243Y (for Municipalities), the Governor of every State shall, within one year from the commencement of the 73rd/74th Amendment Acts and thereafter at the expiration of every fifth year, constitute a Finance Commission to review the financial position of Panchayats/Municipalities. The State Finance Commission (SFC) is constituted every 5 years to: (a) Determine the distribution of taxes between the state and local bodies, (b) Determine grants-in-aid to local bodies from the state's Consolidated Fund, (c) Measures needed to improve the financial position of local bodies. This parallels the Central Finance Commission (constituted under Article 280 every 5 years) at the state level for local body finances.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Indian Constitution confers citizenship by birth but excludes persons born in India to foreign diplomats and enemies.
Reason (R): Citizenship by birth under Article 3 of the Citizenship Act (deriving from Article 5) is an absolute right available to all persons born on Indian soil.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is correct. Under the Citizenship Act, 1955 (as amended), citizenship by birth in India is not available to everyone born in India. Children born to foreign diplomats and those born to enemies of India (during illegal occupation) are excluded. Post-1987 amendments further restricted jus soli (citizenship by birth): persons born in India between January 26, 1950, and July 1, 1987, are citizens regardless of parental nationality. After July 1, 1987: at least one parent must be a citizen. After December 3, 2004: neither parent should be illegal migrant. Reason R is INCORRECT — it incorrectly states citizenship by birth is an 'absolute right' without exclusions. The exclusions for diplomats and enemies make R false. Hence A is true but R is false.

questions.question:
A member of the Rajya Sabha belonging to Party X votes against his party on a crucial bill — the party had issued a three-line whip. Immediately after voting, but before a disqualification notice is served, the member resigns from Party X and joins Party Y. Party X files a disqualification petition with the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

What is the constitutional position?
options: The disqualification proceeding is dropped because the member has already resigned from Party X
The Chairman of Rajya Sabha has jurisdiction to decide the disqualification and the resignation from party does not automatically wipe out prior conduct violating the whip
The Supreme Court must decide all disqualification cases under the Tenth Schedule
Party X's petition fails because resignation from party before disqualification proceedings commence immunizes the member
questions.explanation: Under the Tenth Schedule, the Chairman of Rajya Sabha is the competent authority to decide disqualification petitions for Rajya Sabha members. Key principles: (1) The disqualification relates to conduct (voting against party whip) that occurred when the member was still in Party X — the subsequent resignation does not retroactively validate the whip violation. (2) 'Voluntarily giving up membership' (which triggers disqualification under Para 2(1)(a)) can be inferred from conduct — if the member voted against party whip AND then joined another party, the sequence of events can be considered evidence of giving up membership. (3) The Chairman has original jurisdiction to decide the petition. The Supreme Court reviews Chairman's decision only under Article 32 or Article 136 (judicial review), not as first instance. Option B is correct.

questions.question: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 made important changes to the Council of Ministers. What is the maximum size of the Council of Ministers (including the Prime Minister) as a percentage of the strength of the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 added clause (1A) to Article 75 stating: 'The total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Council of Ministers shall not exceed fifteen per cent of the total strength of the House of the People.' This was inserted to prevent the formation of oversized coalition cabinets ('jumbo cabinets') that had become common in coalition politics. 15% of 543 (Lok Sabha strength) = approximately 81.45, meaning the maximum Council of Ministers size is about 81 ministers. The same provision applies to state governments under Article 164(1A) — maximum 15% of state assembly strength or minimum 12 ministers.

questions.question:
Under Article 360 (Financial Emergency), identify which of the following CANNOT be directed by the President:

1. All money bills passed by State Legislatures must be reserved for Presidential consideration
2. Salaries and allowances of Union employees may be reduced
3. Salaries of judges of Supreme Court and High Courts may be reduced
4. State governments may be directed to dissolve their legislatures immediately
5. States may be directed to observe specific canons of financial propriety
options: 4 only
1 and 4 only
4 and 5 only
None — all five can be directed by the President under Article 360
questions.explanation: Article 360 (Financial Emergency) empowers the President to: Direct states to observe canons of financial propriety (Option 5 — VALID). Reserve all money bills for Presidential consideration (Option 1 — VALID under Article 360(3)). Reduce salaries of government servants including judges of SC and HCs (Options 2 and 3 — VALID under Article 360(4)). However, the President CANNOT direct the dissolution of state legislatures under Article 360. Dissolution of Vidhan Sabha during a Financial Emergency is not provided for under Article 360. The President cannot use Financial Emergency to dissolve state legislatures — this would require a different constitutional provision (Article 356 — President's Rule). Therefore, Option 4 (directing dissolution of state legislatures) CANNOT be done under Article 360. Only Option 4 is incorrect.

questions.question: The 'Right to Information' (RTI) movement and the RTI Act, 2005 have significantly impacted governance. The RTI Act created the Central Information Commission (CIC). The CIC was given the status of a statutory body. Which Fundamental Right is the RTI Act primarily rooted in?
options: Article 14 — Right to Equality
Article 19(1)(a) — Freedom of speech and expression including the right to know
Article 21 — Right to life and personal liberty
Article 32 — Right to constitutional remedies
questions.explanation: The Right to Information is primarily derived from Article 19(1)(a) — freedom of speech and expression. The Supreme Court in a series of judgments (including State of U.P. v. Raj Narain, 1975 and S.P. Gupta v. Union of India, 1982) held that freedom of speech and expression includes the right to know and the right to receive information. Without access to information, citizens cannot meaningfully exercise freedom of expression or participate in democracy. The RTI Act, 2005 operationalized this fundamental right by creating a legal framework for accessing government information. Article 21 (right to life) also supports RTI as informed decision-making is essential for dignified life, but Article 19(1)(a) is the PRIMARY constitutional foundation.

questions.question:
During a session of Parliament, a Member of Parliament makes a speech in the Lok Sabha which is defamatory about a private individual. The aggrieved individual files a defamation suit in a civil court against the MP.

What is the correct constitutional position?
options: The MP can be sued as constitutional protections don't cover defamation
The MP has complete immunity under Article 105(2) — no court proceedings lie for any speech made in Parliament
The MP has immunity only from criminal defamation but not from civil defamation suits
The House can waive the MP's immunity and allow the court proceedings to continue
questions.explanation: Article 105(2) provides: 'No member of Parliament shall be liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said or any vote given by him in Parliament or any committee thereof.' This is an absolute, unqualified privilege. It means: (1) NO court proceedings (civil OR criminal) can be initiated against an MP for anything said in Parliament or a committee. (2) This immunity is absolute — there is no distinction between criminal and civil proceedings. (3) Even if the speech is defamatory, libellous, or false, no external court can take cognisance. (4) Parliament can take disciplinary action internally through its privileges committee, but courts cannot. The rationale is that MPs must be free to speak without fear of external legal consequences to preserve parliamentary freedom.

questions.question: Under Schedule VII of the Indian Constitution, the Union List has 100 subjects (as numbered), the State List has 61 subjects, and the Concurrent List has 52 subjects (as of current count post-various amendments). If a new technology-related subject can plausibly fall under any of these three lists and Parliament passes a law on it invoking the Residuary Powers under Entry 97 of the Union List, which of the following is the correct constitutional basis?
options: Parliament can legislate on it because technology is in the Union List Entry 97 as a specific subject
Parliament can legislate on it under Entry 97 (Union List) which covers any matter not enumerated in any of the three Lists — the Residuary Power
States can legislate on it because technology infrastructure is in the State List
Both Parliament and States can simultaneously legislate as technology is in the Concurrent List
questions.explanation: Entry 97 of the Union List (7th Schedule) is the Residuary Entry which states: 'Any other matter not enumerated in List II or List III including any tax not mentioned in either of those Lists.' This is the Residuary Power vested EXCLUSIVELY in Parliament (Union/Central Government). Article 248 also affirms this: Parliament has exclusive power to make any law with respect to any matter not enumerated in the Concurrent or State List. The Residuary Power follows the pattern of Canada (not USA, where residuary powers vest in States). When a new subject like internet governance, digital currencies, or emerging technologies is not covered by any existing List entry, Parliament can legislate under Entry 97/Article 248. Option B correctly identifies the Residuary Power basis.

questions.question: The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011 dealt with co-operative societies. Which article was amended/inserted regarding the right to form co-operative societies?
options: A new right under Article 19(1)(c) and a new Article 43B was inserted in DPSP
Article 19(1)(g) was amended to include co-operatives
Article 19(1)(c) was amended to include the right to form co-operative societies AND Article 43B was inserted in DPSPs
A new Part IX-B was added for co-operative societies
questions.explanation: The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011 made three-pronged changes: (1) Article 19(1)(c) was amended to add 'co-operative societies' to the existing right to form associations or unions, thus making the right to form co-operatives a Fundamental Right. (2) A new Directive Principle, Article 43B, was inserted requiring the State to endeavour to promote voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control, and professional management of co-operative societies. (3) A new Part IX-B (Articles 243ZH to 243ZT) was added to the Constitution providing for incorporation and regulation of co-operative societies. Note: Part of this amendment relating to state co-operatives was struck down by the Supreme Court in 2021 (Rajendra Shah v. Union of India) as infringing on state autonomy over state co-operatives.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978 significantly reversed many changes made by the 42nd Amendment.
Reason (R): The 44th Amendment restored the right to property as a Fundamental Right which had been deleted by the 42nd Amendment.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — the 44th Amendment (enacted by the Janata government, 1978) substantially reversed several 42nd Amendment changes: restored primacy of Fundamental Rights over DPSPs (Article 31C was narrowed), restored judicial review power, restored 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion' for Article 352 Emergency, required Cabinet advice in writing to President, strengthened protections against Emergency abuse. However, Reason R is INCORRECT — the Right to Property as a Fundamental Right was REMOVED by the 44th Amendment itself, NOT by the 42nd Amendment. The 42nd Amendment had weakened property rights but the 44th Amendment DELETED Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31, converting property right into a mere legal right under new Article 300A. Hence A is true but R is false — R misattributes the deletion of property right to the 42nd Amendment.

questions.question: The 'NOTA' (None Of The Above) option in Indian elections was introduced following a Supreme Court judgment. Which case directed the ECI to provide NOTA on Electronic Voting Machines?
options: People's Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) v. Union of India, 2013
Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India, 2002
Union of India v. Association for Democratic Reforms, 2002
Subramanian Swamy v. Election Commission of India, 2013
questions.explanation: In People's Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) v. Union of India (2013), the Supreme Court held that voters have a fundamental right to express disapproval of all candidates through the NOTA (None Of The Above) option. The court reasoned that the right to vote includes the right to reject, and providing NOTA would: (a) give voice to voters dissatisfied with all candidates, (b) encourage greater voter participation, (c) compel political parties to field better candidates. The ECI subsequently introduced NOTA in the November 2013 state assembly elections. Note that NOTA votes are counted but do not affect the election result — the candidate with the highest votes still wins even if NOTA gets more votes than individual candidates (though ECI has since revised this for local body elections in some states).

questions.question:
Consider the following scenario involving legislative powers during a National Emergency (Article 352 proclaimed on grounds of external aggression):

1. Parliament can make laws on subjects in the State List.
2. Article 19 rights are automatically suspended for citizens.
3. President can, by order, suspend the right of any person to move any court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights under Part III.
4. The Emergency automatically lapses after 2 months unless approved by both Houses.
5. During Emergency, term of Lok Sabha can be extended by 1 year at a time indefinitely.

Identify the INCORRECTLY stated provisions:
options: 3 and 5 only
5 only
2 and 5 only
None — all five are correct
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: During National Emergency, Parliament can legislate on State List subjects (Article 250). Statement 2 — CORRECT: Under Article 358, when Emergency is proclaimed on grounds of war or external aggression, Article 19 is automatically suspended (the restriction is automatic — unlike Article 359 suspension which requires a separate Presidential Order). Statement 3 — CORRECT: Article 359 allows the President to suspend enforcement of other Fundamental Rights (not Articles 20 and 21) by order. Statement 4 — CORRECT: A Proclamation of Emergency must be approved by each House within one month by special majority (2/3 present+voting AND majority of total membership), or it lapses — this is similar to 'after 1 month' requirement — the question states 2 months which is INCORRECT; it's 1 MONTH. Statement 5 — CORRECT that Parliament can extend, but NOT indefinitely — only for 1 year at a time. Statement 4 says 2 months but the Constitution says 1 month, so Statement 4 is incorrect. Among the options, statement 5 is NOT incorrect (1 year at a time is correct), but statement 4 is (should be 1 month not 2 months). Given the options presented, option B (5 only) doesn't match. Let's reconsider: statement 5 says 'indefinitely' which is INCORRECT as there's a 6-month cap after emergency ceases. The most defensible answer is B (5 only) as statement 5 incorrectly says 'indefinitely.'

questions.question: Article 368 of the Indian Constitution lays down the procedure for amendment. In which part of the Constitution is Article 368 located?
options: Part XVIII — Emergency Provisions
Part XIX — Miscellaneous
Part XX — Amendment of the Constitution
Part XXI — Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions
questions.explanation: Article 368 (Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor) is located in Part XX of the Indian Constitution, which is titled 'Amendment of the Constitution.' This Part contains only one Article — Article 368 — which is the sole provision governing the constitutional amendment procedure. Part XVIII (Articles 352–360) covers Emergency Provisions. Part XIX (Articles 361–367) covers Miscellaneous provisions. Part XXI (Articles 369–392) covers Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions. Understanding the structure of the Constitution's Parts is important for both factual recall and analytical questions about the amendment process.

questions.question:
A Union Territory 'UT-Alpha' has a legislature and a Council of Ministers. The Lt. Governor of UT-Alpha acts contrary to the advice of the Council of Ministers on a matter relating to law and order, citing that law and order falls under the Lt. Governor's discretionary domain. The Chief Minister challenges this before the High Court.

This scenario most resembles the constitutional dispute in:
options: Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker (Arunachal Pradesh, 2016) — Governor's powers over assembly session
Government of NCT of Delhi v. Union of India (2018) — LG vs elected government powers
Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India (2006) — Governor's recommendation for President's Rule
Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974) — President/Governor to act on Council of Ministers' advice
questions.explanation: The dispute described — an Lt. Governor acting against the Council of Ministers' advice in a Union Territory with a legislature — directly mirrors the landmark constitutional dispute in Government of NCT of Delhi v. Union of India (2018). A 5-judge constitutional bench of the Supreme Court, in its judgment on July 4, 2018, held that: (1) The LG of Delhi must act on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers on matters within the legislative competence of the Delhi assembly. (2) The LG cannot obstruct or paralyze normal functioning of the elected government. (3) 'Services' was a contested domain (later resolved by Delhi Services case 2023). This case directly addresses the LG vs elected government conflict in a UT with legislature — making Option B the most directly relevant precedent.

questions.question: Article 19(1)(g) guarantees the right to practise any profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business. Which of the following restrictions on this right has been upheld by the Supreme Court as constitutionally valid?
options: A complete ban on tobacco cultivation
Mandatory qualification requirements for practising medicine (MBBS/MD degree)
A prohibition on practising as a lawyer without a law degree from a foreign university
Restrictions on establishing private schools in states with 100% government school enrollment
questions.explanation: Article 19(6) allows the state to impose reasonable restrictions on Article 19(1)(g) in the interest of the general public, or to prescribe professional or technical qualifications necessary for practising any profession or carrying on any occupation, trade or business. Requiring an MBBS/MD degree to practise medicine is a classic example of a valid restriction — it ensures public health and safety and falls squarely within Article 19(6)'s explicit permission for professional qualification requirements. Option A (complete ban) would likely fail the 'reasonable' test unless extreme circumstances exist. Option C (restriction on foreign degrees) needs careful analysis but is more complex. Option D is not a direct restriction on the right to practise. Option B is the clearest and most established example of a valid, constitutionally upheld restriction.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006 derives its constitutional validity from Article 46 (DPSP) relating to promotion of educational and economic interests of STs.
Reason (R): DPSPs, being non-justiciable, cannot provide constitutional validity to any Act of Parliament; such validity must come from the Union List or Concurrent List.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — the Forest Rights Act derives moral and policy legitimacy from Article 46 (DPSP) and also Article 29 (minority cultural rights). More importantly, it derives its legislative competence from Entry 17 (Forests) and Entry 63 (Ancient and historical monuments, tribal areas) of the Concurrent List, and Article 244 (administration of scheduled and tribal areas). Reason R is INCORRECT. DPSPs, while non-justiciable as claims, do have constitutional significance: (1) Parliament can use DPSPs as guidance for legislation (not as the legal basis/legislative competence). (2) Laws implementing DPSPs can be protected under Article 31C from certain FR challenges. (3) DPSPs assist courts in determining the reasonableness of restrictions on Fundamental Rights. R's claim that DPSPs 'cannot provide constitutional validity' oversimplifies — the validity comes from legislative lists, but DPSPs provide interpretive and policy guidance. Hence A is correct but R is incorrect/oversimplified.

questions.question:
In a State Legislature with 200 members, the following scenario arises:
- Party A: 90 members
- Party B: 60 members  
- Party C: 30 members
- Independents: 20 members

Party A forms the government. To pass a Constitutional Amendment Bill (special majority under Article 368), Parliament requires special majority. But for State Legislature ratification under Article 368(2) proviso, the State Legislature must ratify. What is the minimum 'YES' votes needed from this legislature for valid ratification, assuming quorum is present?
options: 101 votes (majority of total = more than 100)
The state legislature ratification requires only simple majority — so 101 votes
State legislature ratification for Article 368 requires special majority — 2/3 of present+voting AND majority of total
questions.explanation: Article 368(2) proviso states that for certain Constitutional Amendments, 'ratification by the Legislatures of not less than one-half of the States by resolutions to that effect passed by those Legislatures' is required. The Constitution specifies the METHOD of ratification but does not explicitly state the majority required within each state legislature for passing the ratification resolution. The standard constitutional interpretation, as debated by scholars and supported by various state assembly precedents, is that the ratification resolution requires a SIMPLE MAJORITY in the state legislature (majority of members present and voting with quorum). The special majority (2/3 present+voting AND majority of total) applies in PARLIAMENT under Article 368, not in state legislatures for ratification. Therefore, the minimum votes needed = simple majority = 101 (more than half of 200, assuming all 200 are present and voting). Option C most accurately reflects this position.

questions.question: The concept of 'Checks and Balances' in Indian democracy is demonstrated by which of the following constitutional mechanisms?
options: Parliament cannot pass any law without judicial approval from the Supreme Court
The Supreme Court can strike down laws passed by Parliament if they violate the Constitution; Parliament can override this only by constitutional amendment that does not violate Basic Structure
The President must consult the Chief Justice before all executive decisions
The Council of Ministers must seek judicial approval before implementing policies
questions.explanation: The Indian system of checks and balances is best illustrated by the dynamic between judicial review and parliamentary power: (1) Parliament passes laws — these are subject to judicial review by the Supreme Court under Articles 32, 131-136, 143, 226 etc. (2) The Supreme Court can strike down laws that violate Fundamental Rights, constitutional provisions, or (post-Kesavananda Bharati, 1973) the Basic Structure. (3) Parliament can respond by constitutional amendment — but such amendments cannot destroy the Basic Structure (which is itself guarded by the judiciary). This creates a sophisticated equilibrium. Options A and C are clearly wrong — there is no prior judicial approval for laws or executive decisions. Option D is also wrong. Option B accurately captures the constitutional check that judicial review provides against parliamentary overreach, balanced by Parliament's amendment power.

questions.question:
The Indian Constitution provides for reservation of seats for SCs and STs in Parliament and State Legislatures under Article 330-332. Currently, such reservation exists until:

Statement A: The reservation for SCs and STs in Parliament automatically expired in 1970.
Statement B: Each successive constitutional amendment has extended the reservation.
Statement C: The latest extension was made by the 104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019, extending reservation until January 25, 2030.

Which of the above statements are correct?
options: Statement A only
Statements B and C only
Statement C only
All three are correct
questions.explanation: Statement A is INCORRECT — the reservation for SCs and STs in Parliament did not expire in 1970. The original Constitution provided for reservation for 10 years from the commencement of the Constitution (i.e., until 1960). Statement B is CORRECT — each subsequent constitutional amendment has extended the period of reservation. Statement C is CORRECT — the 104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019 extended the reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the House of the People and in the Legislative Assemblies of the States for another 10 years — until January 25, 2030. It was also this same 104th Amendment that abolished the provision for nomination of Anglo-Indian members. Hence Statements B and C are correct.

questions.question:
A political party wins a state election with a clear majority. During government formation, the Governor invites another party claiming there is doubt about post-election alliances. The winning party approaches the Supreme Court.

Based on constitutional principles and precedent, which statement BEST describes the Court's likely response?
options: The Governor has complete discretion in government formation and courts cannot interfere

The Court will apply the S.R. Bommai principle that the floor of the Assembly is the proper place to test majority, and may direct the Governor to invite the single largest party to form government and prove majority on the floor
The Court will dissolve the assembly and order fresh elections
The Court will appoint a caretaker government until the dispute is resolved
questions.explanation: Based on the S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994) principles and subsequent case law (including Shivraj Singh Chouhan v. Speaker, Madhya Pradesh Assembly, 2020), the Supreme Court has consistently held: (1) The floor of the Assembly is the only place to test majority. (2) Governors cannot use subjective satisfaction to bypass clear electoral mandates. (3) When there is a clear single largest party, the Governor should invite them to form government. (4) Gubernatorial actions in government formation are subject to judicial review. In Goa Governor case and similar situations, the Supreme Court has directed governors to invite the single largest pre-poll alliance/party and has set tight deadlines for floor tests. The Court cannot dissolve assemblies or appoint caretakers — those are not within judicial power. Option B is correct.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right was described by the Supreme Court as the 'golden triangle' along with Articles 14 and 19?
options: Article 20 — Protection against self-incrimination
Article 21 — Protection of life and personal liberty
Article 22 — Protection against arrest and detention
Article 23 — Prohibition of forced labour
questions.explanation: Articles 14 (Equality before law), 19 (Six freedoms), and 21 (Right to life and personal liberty) are collectively referred to as the 'Golden Triangle' of the Indian Constitution. This term was coined because together these three articles form the most essential core of Fundamental Rights that no person can be deprived of arbitrarily. The Maneka Gandhi case (1978) significantly expanded the interpretation of Article 21 and established that any law depriving a person of their life or liberty must satisfy all three provisions — the procedure prescribed must be fair, just, and reasonable (not merely any procedure). This made the 'golden triangle' approach the cornerstone of constitutional rights analysis in India.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Indian Constitution is federal in structure with unitary features.
Reason (R): In India, there is a strong centre with clearly defined powers, states are provided residual powers, and the Constitution cannot be amended without the concurrence of all state governments.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — India's Constitution is described as 'quasi-federal' or federal with strong unitary features (as described by Dr. K.C. Wheare as a 'quasi-federal state'). However, Reason R contains significant ERRORS: (1) In India, residuary powers vest in PARLIAMENT/UNION (Entry 97, Union List), NOT in states. (2) Constitutional amendments do NOT require concurrence of ALL state governments — only specific amendments require ratification by at least HALF the states (not all). Most amendments can be made by Parliament alone. These are precisely the UNITARY features of India's Constitution. Therefore, R contains factual errors that mischaracterize the Indian federal system. A is true but R is false.

questions.question:
Study the following data on Constitutional Amendments:

Category                          | Number of Amendments (approx.)
Amendments related to 7th Schedule | 19
Amendments extending reservation   | 7
Amendments related to elections     | 11
Amendments related to DPSP/FR       | 9
Amendments for administrative matters | 49

Total Constitutional Amendments as of 2024 ≈ 106

Which of the following inferences is MOST analytically valid?
options: Most Constitutional Amendments have been made to the Fundamental Rights chapter showing its instability
The high number of amendments related to administrative matters shows that the Constitution has evolved as a working document adapting to governance needs, rather than being a rigid document
The Constitution has been amended so frequently that it has lost its original character and basic structure
The 7th Schedule amendments show that states frequently cede powers to the Centre
questions.explanation: The data shows that the largest category of amendments (49) relates to administrative matters — creation of new states, changes in state boundaries, extension of certain provisions, inclusion of languages in 8th Schedule, etc. This suggests the Constitution has served as a living document that adapts to India's evolving administrative and political reality. Option A is incorrect — the data doesn't show most amendments were to Fundamental Rights (only 9 of ~106). Option C is analytically unfounded — while the Constitution has been amended frequently, the Basic Structure Doctrine (1973) has preserved its essential character and the Supreme Court has struck down amendments violating basic structure. Option D is incorrect — 7th Schedule amendments don't necessarily mean states cede powers; they also reflect reorganization of entries. Option B is the most valid inference.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution empowers the President to promulgate ordinances when Parliament is not in session, and what is the MAXIMUM validity of an ordinance if Parliament does not act on it?
options: Article 122; 6 weeks from reassembly of Parliament
Article 123; 6 weeks from reassembly of the first of the two Houses to convene
Article 123; 3 months from date of promulgation regardless of Parliament's session
Article 124; 3 months from date of promulgation if Parliament disapproves
questions.explanation: Article 123 empowers the President to promulgate Ordinances when both Houses of Parliament are not in session (or when at least one House is not in session). The Ordinance has the same force and effect as an Act of Parliament. As to its validity: An Ordinance automatically ceases to operate upon the expiry of six weeks from the date of reassembly of Parliament (starting from the date the first House reassembles — not when both Houses have assembled). It will also cease to operate earlier if resolutions disapproving the Ordinance are passed by both Houses. The correct provision is Article 123 (not 122 or 124), and the 6-week period runs from the reassembly of the first House, making Option B correct.

questions.question: The Central Government proposes to create a new All-India Service for health professionals. The proposal faces opposition from several state governments. Under what constitutional provision can Parliament create the new service, and what approval mechanism is required?
options: Parliament can create it unilaterally under Entry 70 of the Union List (Union Services)
Parliament can create it under Article 312 only if a resolution is passed by Rajya Sabha by not less than 2/3rd of members present and voting declaring it necessary in the national interest
Only the states where the service will operate need to give consent through their respective legislatures
The President can create such a service by executive order without requiring Parliamentary approval
questions.explanation: Article 312 of the Indian Constitution specifically provides the mechanism for creation of new All India Services. It states: Notwithstanding anything in Chapter VI of Part VI or Part XI, if the Council of States has declared by resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest to do so, Parliament may by law provide for the creation of one or more All India Services common to the Union and the States. This is a unique constitutional mechanism where the Rajya Sabha (Council of States — representing states) must first pass such a resolution by a 2/3rd supermajority, after which Parliament can legislate. The IAS, IPS, and Indian Forest Service are the three existing All India Services. Option B correctly states the Article 312 requirement.

questions.question: Who presides over the joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament under Article 108?
options: The President of India
The Vice-President (Chairman of Rajya Sabha)
The Speaker of the Lok Sabha
The senior-most member of either House
questions.explanation: Under Article 118 read with Article 108, the Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament. Article 118(4) specifically provides that the Speaker of the House of the People shall preside at a joint sitting of the two Houses. In the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides. In the absence of both, the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha presides. The Lok Sabha Speaker's presiding role over joint sittings reflects the constitutional recognition of Lok Sabha's primary legislative role (being the directly elected house). Joint sittings have been convened three times in Indian history: Dowry Prohibition Act (1961), Banking Service Commission Repeal Bill (1977), and Prevention of Terrorism Act/POTA (2002).

questions.question:
A five-judge bench of the Supreme Court passes a judgment by 3:2 majority. One of the 3 judges writes a concurring opinion with different reasoning, while 2 judges dissent. A similar case comes before a 3-judge bench.

Under Article 141, which judicial opinion is binding on the 3-judge bench?
options: The majority opinion (3 judges' reasoning) including the concurring opinion's reasoning
Only the operative order (disposition) of the 3:2 majority; the reasoning of the majority is not binding if the 3 judges had different reasoning
The dissenting opinion should be followed if it is more reasoned
The concurring opinion alone as it represents the middle ground
questions.explanation: Under Article 141, the law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within India. However, the concept of ratio decidendi (the reason for the decision) is crucial. When a 5-judge bench decides by 3:2, but 2 of the 3 majority judges have one reasoning while 1 majority judge (concurring) has different reasoning: (1) The OPERATIVE ORDER/DISPOSITION is binding on all courts. (2) For the reasoning (ratio) to be binding, there must be a majority in support of a specific legal proposition. If the 3 majority judges had different reasoning paths, it may be difficult to identify a clear ratio. Courts have grappled with 'plurality judgments.' The 3-judge bench is bound by the OUTCOME/ORDER, but must carefully analyze whether a clear ratio from the majority reasoning can be extracted. This is the Carey doctrine applied in Indian jurisprudence. Option B is the most accurate answer.

questions.question: The 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016 introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework. Which of the following was a KEY constitutional change made by this amendment?
options: It moved 'taxes on goods and services' from the State List to the Union List exclusively
It inserted a new Article 246A giving both Parliament and State Legislatures concurrent power to levy GST, with Parliament having exclusive power over inter-state GST
It created a new List IV (GST List) in the 7th Schedule for goods and services taxation
It made GST a Fundamental Right of businesses to obtain input tax credit
questions.explanation: The 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016 made several key changes to implement GST: (1) Inserted Article 246A — a new article giving both Parliament and State Legislatures power to make laws with respect to GST imposed by the respective government. Parliament has exclusive power to legislate on inter-state GST (IGST). This created a new category of concurrent taxation power specifically for GST. (2) Inserted Article 269A — providing that IGST shall be levied and collected by the Government of India and shall be apportioned between the Union and States. (3) The GST Council (Article 279A) was constituted to recommend rates and other matters. The amendment did NOT create a separate List IV, nor did it move anything exclusively to the Union List. Option B correctly describes the concurrent GST legislative power framework.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A Panchayat, if dissolved by the state government before its term, must hold fresh elections within 6 months, and the new Panchayat serves the REMAINDER of the original term.
Reason (R): This provision ensures stability of local government and prevents state governments from using premature dissolution as a political tool to reset the entire 5-year term in their favour.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both Assertion A and Reason R are correct, and R explains the policy rationale behind A. Assertion A correctly states the constitutional provision under Article 243E: fresh elections within 6 months of dissolution, with the new Panchayat serving only the remaining term. Reason R correctly identifies the policy rationale: if a newly constituted Panchayat after early dissolution were to serve a FRESH 5-year term, state governments could dissolve Panchayats dominated by opposition and use fresh elections to install their preferred candidates who would then serve full 5-year terms. By limiting the new Panchayat to the REMAINDER of the original term, the Constitution disincentivizes state governments from using dissolution as a political instrument. R does correctly explain why the provision in A exists. Option A is the correct choice.

questions.question:
The Indian Constitution provides for different types of majorities for different purposes. Match the following:

Purpose                                    | Required Majority
I. Removal of Vice-President               | A. Simple majority (members present+voting)
II. Constitutional Amendment (most Articles)| B. Effective majority (majority of total strength)
III. Removal of Speaker                    | C. Absolute majority (>50% of total membership)
IV. Passing of ordinary bills              | D. Special majority (2/3 present+voting + majority of total)

Which matching is CORRECT?
options: I-B, II-D, III-B, IV-A
I-D, II-B, III-C, IV-A
I-B, II-D, III-C, IV-A
I-C, II-D, III-B, IV-A
questions.explanation: Correct matching: I. Removal of Vice-President (Article 67): By a resolution of the Rajya Sabha passed by a majority of ALL THEN MEMBERS (effective majority = majority of total then-membership, not nominal strength) and agreed to by Lok Sabha — this is an effective majority = B. II. Constitutional Amendment (most articles under Article 368): Requires 2/3rd of members present and voting AND majority of total membership = Special majority = D. III. Removal of Speaker (Article 94): By a resolution of Lok Sabha 'passed by a majority of all the then members' — This is effective majority (majority of total then-membership), not mere simple majority = B. Wait — both Speaker removal and VP removal require effective majority. Let me reconsider: Actually Speaker removal also requires effective majority. Given the options, I-B, II-D, III-B, IV-A (Option A) is closest, but III could also be B. Option A (I-B, II-D, III-B, IV-A) correctly matches all four. Note: 'Effective majority' = majority of total actual strength (vacancies excluded).

questions.question: The 'Doctrine of Severability' in Indian Constitutional Law means:
options: Courts can sever (separate) the constitutional amendment from the original Constitution and test them independently
If a part of a law is unconstitutional, only that part is struck down and the rest remains valid, provided the remaining part is severable and can stand independently
Courts can sever the factual questions from legal questions in constitutional cases
Parliament can sever (exempt) certain laws from judicial review by placing them in Schedule 9
questions.explanation: The Doctrine of Severability (also called the Doctrine of Separability) holds that if a portion of a statute is unconstitutional (violating Fundamental Rights or other constitutional provisions), only that portion is struck down — the remaining valid portions continue to operate, provided: (a) The unconstitutional part is severable from the rest; (b) The remaining part can stand independently and make sense without the severed portion; and (c) Parliament would have enacted the remaining part even without the struck-down portion. This doctrine is recognized under Article 13 (laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights are void 'to the extent of such inconsistency') — the phrase 'to the extent' implies severability. Key cases include R.M.D. Chamarbaugwala v. Union of India (1957) which laid down the test for severability.

questions.question:
Parliament passes a law creating Special Courts for speedy trial of politicians accused of serious crimes. The law provides that once a chargesheet is filed, the accused politician must vacate their seat in Parliament/State Legislature within 90 days or face automatic disqualification.

Which constitutional provision does this law most likely conflict with?
options: Article 105 — Parliamentary privileges and immunities
Article 102 read with the Representation of the People Act, 1951 — which prescribes specific disqualification grounds and requires conviction, not mere chargesheet
Article 20 — Protection against arbitrary conviction without trial
Article 14 — Differential treatment of politicians vs ordinary citizens is arbitrary
questions.explanation: The existing constitutional framework (Article 102(1)(e)) and Representation of the People Act, 1951 (Section 8) provide that disqualification for criminal offences arises only upon CONVICTION and sentence of 2+ years of imprisonment. The mere filing of a chargesheet does not disqualify an MP/MLA — this is a fundamental principle of innocent until proven guilty. A law creating automatic disqualification upon chargesheet would conflict with Article 102 read with RPA, 1951. The Supreme Court in Lily Thomas v. Union of India (2013) struck down Section 8(4) RPA (which allowed convicted MPs to continue if they filed an appeal) and reinforced that conviction itself is the trigger. Creating a new disqualification trigger (chargesheet) would require a constitutional amendment, not merely an ordinary statute, as it alters disqualification provisions contrary to the constitutional scheme.

questions.question: The 'Zero Hour' in Indian Parliament is:
options: The first hour of Parliamentary business when the Speaker addresses the House
An unscheduled period during which members can raise urgent matters without prior notice, typically starting at 12 noon
The hour when Parliament votes on the budget
The period when the President addresses Parliament
questions.explanation: The Zero Hour is an informal parliamentary device used in the Indian Parliament. It starts at 12 noon (hence 'zero hour' — the time between morning session questions and the day's listed business). During Zero Hour, members can raise matters of urgent public importance without prior notice to the Speaker. This is an Indian innovation — it does not exist in the British Parliament from where many Indian parliamentary conventions were borrowed. The term was coined in the early 1960s. Zero Hour typically lasts 1 hour and is preceded by Question Hour (11 AM to 12 noon). It gives opposition members a platform to raise urgent issues that cannot wait for scheduled debate time. There are no formal rules governing Zero Hour in the Rules of Procedure.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The power to issue Directions under Article 256 and Article 257 enables the Central Government to direct State Governments to ensure compliance with Central laws and to not impede Central executive functions.
Reason (R): If a State Government fails to comply with such directions, the Central Government can exercise power under Article 356 (President's Rule) immediately.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT. Articles 256 and 257 provide for executive directions from the Centre to States: Article 256 requires state governments to ensure compliance with parliamentary laws. Article 257 enables the Centre to direct states regarding maintenance of communication means of national importance, protection of railways, etc. Reason R contains a partially correct but misleadingly stated position. Article 365 states that where a State fails to comply with or give effect to any directions given under Part VI, it shall be lawful for the President to hold that a situation has arisen in which the Government of the State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution — thereby enabling Article 356. However, Article 356 application is NOT 'immediate' — it requires the President's judgment (advised by Cabinet) and must comply with S.R. Bommai principles. R overstates by saying 'immediately.' Hence A is correct but R is not fully correct as stated.

questions.question:
In India, the State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) is an upper house of the state legislature. Consider the following:

1. All Indian states must have a Vidhan Parishad (Legislative Council).
2. A Vidhan Parishad can be created or abolished by Parliament on a resolution by the concerned State Vidhan Sabha.
3. A Vidhan Parishad member serves a term of 6 years.
4. The minimum number of members in a Vidhan Parishad is 40.
5. In states with Vidhan Parishad, the Council of Ministers is responsible to both Houses.

Identify the INCORRECT statements:
options: 1 and 5 only
1, 4 and 5 only
1, 3 and 5 only
1 and 3 only
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — INCORRECT: Not all states need to have a Vidhan Parishad. Only 6 states currently have it (Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Bihar, UP). It is optional. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Under Article 169, Parliament can create or abolish a state legislative council if the state's Vidhan Sabha passes a resolution to that effect by a special majority (majority of total membership + 2/3rd of present and voting). Statement 3 — CORRECT: Members serve 6-year terms. Statement 4 — CORRECT: The minimum strength is 40 members (unless Parliament provides otherwise). Maximum is 1/3rd of total Vidhan Sabha members. Statement 5 — INCORRECT: Even in states with both Houses, the Council of Ministers is responsible ONLY to the Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha), NOT to the Council (Vidhan Parishad). This is similar to the Central government being responsible only to Lok Sabha. Hence Statements 1 and 5 are incorrect — Option A is correct.

questions.question: Article 51 of the Indian Constitution relates to 'Promotion of international peace and security.' This article is part of:
options: Part III — Fundamental Rights
Part IV — Directive Principles of State Policy
Part IVA — Fundamental Duties
Part VII — States in Part B of the First Schedule
questions.explanation: Article 51 is part of Part IV (Directive Principles of State Policy), specifically dealing with international relations and peace. Article 51 directs the State to endeavour to: (a) promote international peace and security, (b) maintain just and honourable relations between nations, (c) foster respect for international law and treaty obligations, and (d) encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration. This article is significant as it provides the constitutional basis for India's foreign policy principles, including Panchsheel (five principles of peaceful coexistence) and India's consistent support for the UN and international arbitration. Being a DPSP, it is not justiciable but guides foreign policy formulation.

questions.question:
The following table shows women's representation in Lok Sabha over the years:

Lok Sabha | Year | Women MPs | Total Seats | % Women
5th       | 1971 | 22        | 521         | 4.2%
8th       | 1984 | 44        | 543         | 8.1%
11th      | 1996 | 40        | 543         | 7.4%
14th      | 2004 | 45        | 543         | 8.3%
17th      | 2019 | 78        | 543         | 14.4%

Based on this data and the 106th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2023 (Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam), which analytical conclusion is MOST VALID?
options: Women's representation has been increasing steadily and will naturally reach 33% without constitutional reservation
Despite some improvement, women's representation has been historically low and the 106th Amendment's 33% reservation addresses this structural under-representation
The 17th Lok Sabha's 14.4% figure proves that reservations are unnecessary as organic growth is occurring
Women's political participation is determined solely by social factors and not by constitutional provisions
questions.explanation: The data shows historically low women's representation — ranging from 4.2% to 14.4% over five decades. Even the highest (17th LS at 14.4%) is far below 33%. The 106th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2023 (Constitution (One Hundred and Sixth Amendment) Act, 2023 — Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam) provides for 33% reservation of seats in Lok Sabha, State Assemblies, and Delhi Assembly for women (including seats reserved for SCs/STs). The reservation is to take effect after the next census and delimitation exercise. Option A is incorrect (data shows plateau effect around 7-14%, not steady march toward 33%). Option B is most analytically valid — the structural under-representation evidenced by 50+ years of data justifies the constitutional reservation. Options C and D are analytically flawed.

questions.question: The Supreme Court in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) revolutionized the interpretation of Article 21. What was the key contribution of this judgment?
options: It held that the right to travel abroad is a Fundamental Right under Article 21
It interpreted 'procedure established by law' in Article 21 to mean not just any procedure but a procedure that is fair, just, and reasonable — incorporating due process of law
It held that Article 21 applies even to non-citizens of India
It held that Article 21 cannot be suspended even during a National Emergency
questions.explanation: The Maneka Gandhi case (1978) was a watershed moment for Article 21 interpretation. Prior to this, in A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950), the Supreme Court had held that 'procedure established by law' simply meant any procedure laid down by a competent legislature, however arbitrary. Maneka Gandhi overruled this narrow interpretation. Justice P.N. Bhagwati held that the procedure must be right, just, and fair — not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. This effectively read 'due process of law' into Article 21. The court also held that Articles 14, 19, and 21 must be read together (Golden Triangle) and cannot be interpreted in isolation. The right to travel abroad was found to be part of Article 21, but the KEY contribution was the transformation of 'procedure established by law' to include fairness, justice, and reasonableness — Option B.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution was drafted by the Constituent Assembly. If the Drafting Committee had 7 members and it took 2 years, 11 months and 18 days to complete the Constitution, what were the dates of the Constituent Assembly's first sitting and the Constitution's adoption?
options: First sitting: December 9, 1946; Adoption: November 26, 1949
First sitting: January 26, 1947; Adoption: November 26, 1949
First sitting: December 9, 1946; Adoption: January 26, 1950
First sitting: August 15, 1947; Adoption: November 26, 1949
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly of India had its first sitting on December 9, 1946 (under the presidency of Dr. Sachidananda Sinha, the temporary Chairman). Dr. Rajendra Prasad was elected as the permanent President on December 11, 1946. The Constitution was finally ADOPTED on November 26, 1949 (celebrated as Constitution Day/Samvidhan Diwas). However, the Constitution came into FORCE on January 26, 1950 (celebrated as Republic Day). The calculation from December 9, 1946 to November 26, 1949 ≈ 2 years, 11 months, 17 days (close to the stated '2 years, 11 months and 18 days'). The Drafting Committee was chaired by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and had 7 members. The Constitution underwent 2,473 amendments to the draft before final adoption.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Under the Indian Constitution, the judiciary has the power of judicial review over both legislative and executive actions.
Reason (R): This power flows from Article 32 (SC), Article 226 (HC), and Articles 13, 131-136 and 141 which together create the framework for constitutional judicial review.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are correct and R provides a comprehensive explanation of A. A is correct — the Indian Supreme Court and High Courts have extensive power of judicial review over both legislative and executive actions. R correctly identifies the constitutional provisions: Article 13 (laws inconsistent with FRs are void — basis for legislative review), Article 32 (SC's power to enforce FRs — basis for executive and legislative review through writs), Article 226 (HC's writ jurisdiction — broader than SC in scope), Articles 131-136 (original and appellate jurisdiction of SC), Article 141 (law declared by SC is binding). These provisions collectively create India's judicial review framework. Article 32 enables review of executive actions violating FRs; Article 13 enables striking down inconsistent laws; Articles 131-136 provide the jurisdictional basis. R correctly and completely explains A.

questions.question:
Consider the following features of the Indian Constitution and identify those that make India a 'Republic':

1. The Head of State (President) is an elected position, not a hereditary one.
2. No permanent hereditary aristocracy — no titles of nobility that are heritable.
3. The President is elected for a fixed term of 5 years.
4. All citizens are equal before law irrespective of birth or title.
5. The Constitution derives its authority from 'We, the People' as stated in the Preamble.

Which of the above features DIRECTLY make India a Republic?
options: 1 and 3 only
1, 2 and 3 only
1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 — all contribute to Republican character
1, 3 and 5 only
questions.explanation: A 'Republic' in constitutional terms means: (a) The head of state is elected (not hereditary/appointed), (b) No permanent aristocratic class, (c) Power derives from the people. All five features contribute to India's Republican character: Feature 1 — Elected President directly establishes Republican form. Feature 2 — Article 18 abolishes titles (except military/academic); preventing hereditary aristocracy is essential to republicanism. Feature 3 — Fixed term prevents permanent rule. Feature 4 — Equality before law is essential to republican ideals. Feature 5 — Preamble's 'We, the People' doctrine of popular sovereignty is the foundation of republicanism. As opposed to a monarchy (hereditary, permanent) or oligarchy, India's republic is defined by all these features collectively. Option C (all five) is correct.

questions.question:
Parliament passes the 'Freedom of Religion Protection Act' which prohibits forcible religious conversions and requires prior government approval for all conversions. A minority religious community challenges this law arguing it violates Articles 25-28.

Which of the following analyses is MOST constitutionally accurate?
options: The law is clearly unconstitutional as no restrictions can be placed on religious conversions

Article 25(1) protects the right to propagate religion but the Supreme Court has held that the right to propagate does not include the right to convert another person — anti-conversion laws requiring voluntary nature are valid, but prior government approval may raise Article 14 and Article 25 concerns
The law is valid because religious conversions affect public order and are thus completely within state power to regulate
Anti-conversion laws always violate Article 25 as propagation of religion is absolute
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court in Rev. Stainislaus v. State of M.P. (1977) upheld the validity of state anti-conversion laws (like the M.P. Dharma Swatantraya Adhiniyam) and held that Article 25(1) guarantees the right to propagate religion but does NOT include the right to convert another person by use of force, fraud, or allurement.
