The right to propagate means the right to spread one's religious beliefs peacefully — not to convert. Therefore, laws preventing forcible/fraudulent conversions are constitutionally valid. However, requiring PRIOR GOVERNMENT APPROVAL for ALL conversions (including purely voluntary ones) raises serious concerns: (a) Article 25 protects the right to convert if the person consents freely, (b) Prior approval may violate Article 14 (arbitrary and discriminatory), and (c) It may amount to excessive state control over religious practice. Option B correctly captures this nuanced constitutional position.

questions.question: The National Emergency declared in 1975 by President Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed on the advice of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was proclaimed under which Article and on what grounds?
options: Article 352, on grounds of external aggression from Pakistan
Article 352, on grounds of internal disturbance (the original language before 44th Amendment)
Article 356, due to constitutional breakdown
Article 360, due to threat to financial stability
questions.explanation: The National Emergency of 1975 (June 25, 1975 to March 21, 1977) was proclaimed under Article 352 on the grounds of 'internal disturbance' — the original language used in Article 352 before it was replaced by 'armed rebellion' by the 44th Constitutional Amendment, 1978. This was a controversial proclamation as many jurists and opposition leaders argued there was no genuine 'internal disturbance' warranting emergency. It was the third National Emergency (after 1962 during Chinese war and 1971 during Bangladesh Liberation War). The 1975 Emergency's misuse led to the 44th Amendment, 1978 which: changed 'internal disturbance' to 'armed rebellion,' required Cabinet recommendation in writing to the President, and mandated fresh approval of each House within 1 month.

questions.question: The State Election Commission (SEC) established under Article 243K is responsible for superintendence, direction, and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and conduct of elections to Panchayats and Municipalities. The SEC is:
options: Part of the Election Commission of India (ECI) and operates under ECI's supervision
A separate constitutional body independent of the ECI, with the State Election Commissioner appointed by the Governor
A statutory body established by state legislation, not by the Constitution
Appointed by the Chief Minister of the state on the advice of the state Cabinet
questions.explanation: Article 243K establishes the State Election Commission as a SEPARATE constitutional body, independent of the Election Commission of India (which handles only Parliamentary, Presidential, and VP elections). Key features: (1) The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the GOVERNOR of the State. (2) The State EC is independent of the ECI — it operates separately for Panchayat and Municipal elections. (3) The State Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same manner and on the same grounds as a High Court Judge (i.e., on an address of the state legislature by the required majority, presented to the Governor). (4) After appointment, the conditions of service of the State EC cannot be varied to their disadvantage. The SEC has the same autonomy for local body elections as the ECI has for parliamentary elections.

questions.question:
Consider rights under Articles 23 and 24 (Right against Exploitation):

Statement 1: Article 23 prohibits human trafficking and beggar (forced labour without payment), but allows the State to impose compulsory service for public purposes.

Statement 2: Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or hazardous employment — but NOT in family enterprises or domestic work.

Statement 3: Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976 was enacted to give effect to Article 23 and makes all bonded labour agreements void ab initio.

Statement 4: The Child and Adolescent Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986 completely prohibits children under 18 from ALL forms of employment without exception.

Which statements are CORRECTLY stated?
options: 1 and 3 only
1, 2 and 3 only
2, 3 and 4 only
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: Article 23 prohibits human trafficking and forced labour (begar). However, Article 23(2) explicitly allows the State to impose compulsory service for public purposes (like national service, community service) provided it does not discriminate on grounds of religion, race, caste or class. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Article 24 prohibits employment in factories, mines, or 'any other hazardous employment.' Family enterprises (non-hazardous) and domestic work were traditionally not covered. (Note: The Child Labour Amendment Act 2016 has since broadened restrictions.) Statement 3 — CORRECT: The Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976 nullifies all bonded labour obligations and makes such agreements void. Statement 4 — INCORRECT: The Child and Adolescent Labour Act does not prohibit ALL employment for children under 18. Children under 14 can work in family-run non-hazardous enterprises. The 2016 Amendment created categories: children (under 14) and adolescents (14-18) with different restrictions. Hence Statements 1, 2, and 3 are correct.

questions.question: The Preamble declares India to be a 'Sovereign' state. In contemporary international law, which of the following CORRECTLY explains what 'Sovereign' means in the context of the Indian Constitution?
options: India can make any law it wishes without any international obligations

India is internally and externally supreme — free from control of any other state and can conduct its own foreign policy, enter treaties, and is recognized by international law as a subject of sovereign rights
India's sovereignty means it cannot be a member of any international organization that places restrictions on its conduct
Sovereignty means the President has unlimited power within the territory of India
questions.explanation: Sovereignty in the constitutional context means: EXTERNAL SOVEREIGNTY — India is free from control or domination of any foreign power. It is recognized as an independent nation by the international community, can sign treaties, join international organizations, declare war and peace, etc. INTERNAL SOVEREIGNTY — India has supreme power within its territory; no external authority can override Indian law within India. The term 'Sovereign' in the Preamble was included to clarify that India is fully independent (not a dominion under Britain) and has complete authority. Option A is incorrect — sovereignty doesn't mean India ignores international law; India is bound by treaty obligations it voluntarily assumes. Option C is wrong — sovereignty is compatible with membership in international organizations (UN, WTO, etc.). Option D is wrong — sovereignty doesn't mean the President has unlimited power.

questions.question: A High Court judge is found to have delivered judgments in exchange for bribes. The Chief Justice of India receives complaints. Under the existing constitutional framework, what is the procedure for the removal of a High Court judge?
options: The Chief Justice of India can directly remove the judge after an in-house inquiry
The President removes the HC judge on the recommendation of the concerned High Court's collegium

The Parliament passes an address to the President praying for removal, supported by majority of total membership and 2/3rd of present and voting in each House, after which the President removes the judge
The Governor of the concerned state removes the HC judge after consulting the High Court's Chief Justice
questions.explanation: Under Article 217 read with Article 124(4), a High Court judge can be removed only by an order of the President passed after an address by each House of Parliament supported by: (a) A majority of the TOTAL MEMBERSHIP of each House, and (b) A majority of NOT LESS THAN TWO-THIRDS of the members of each House PRESENT AND VOTING. The grounds are proven misbehaviour or incapacity. The procedure follows the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968: (1) A notice signed by 100 Lok Sabha members or 50 Rajya Sabha members is given; (2) A committee of three is formed (CJI + HC CJ + distinguished jurist); (3) If committee finds proven misbehaviour, Parliament passes the address; (4) President issues removal order. The CJI cannot unilaterally remove a HC judge. The Veeraswami case and other precedents affirm this constitutional process.

questions.question: Which Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains the Oath of Office for the President, Vice-President, Members of Parliament, Judges of Supreme Court and High Courts, and other constitutional functionaries?
options: First Schedule
Second Schedule
Third Schedule
Fourth Schedule
questions.explanation: The Third Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains the forms of Oaths or Affirmations to be taken by various constitutional functionaries. These include: (1) Union Ministers, (2) Candidates for election to Parliament, (3) Members of Parliament, (4) Judges of Supreme Court, (5) Comptroller and Auditor General of India, (6) Candidates for election to State Legislature, (7) Members of State Legislature, (8) Ministers for a State, (9) Judges of High Courts. The First Schedule lists the States and Union Territories. The Second Schedule covers salaries and emoluments of various constitutional functionaries (President, Governors, Judges of SC and HC, CAG, etc.). The Fourth Schedule allocates seats in the Rajya Sabha to States and Union Territories.

questions.question:
Consider the provisions relating to the office of the Attorney General of India (Article 76):

1. The Attorney General is appointed by the President and is the highest law officer of the country.
2. The AG must have qualifications to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court.
3. The AG holds office during the pleasure of the President.
4. The AG has the right of audience in all courts throughout India.
5. The AG is a member of the Council of Ministers.

Which of the above statements are INCORRECT?
options: 5 only
3 and 5 only
2, 3 and 5 only
None — all five are correct
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: The AG is appointed by the President under Article 76 and is India's highest law officer. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Under Article 76(1), the AG must be a person qualified to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court (i.e., has been a judge of HC for 5 years OR an advocate of HC for 10 years, OR is a distinguished jurist in the President's opinion). Statement 3 — CORRECT: Article 76(4) states the AG holds office during the pleasure of the President. Statement 4 — CORRECT: Article 76(3) gives the AG the right of audience in all courts throughout India. Statement 5 — INCORRECT: The AG is NOT a member of the Council of Ministers. The AG is a constitutional appointee who gives legal advice to the Government of India and appears on behalf of the Union in courts — but is not a cabinet minister. The AG is not a member of Parliament and does not have voting rights in Parliament (though they have the right to speak in any Parliamentary proceeding). Hence only Statement 5 is incorrect.

questions.question: The Representation of the People Act, 1951 (RPA) lays down qualifications and disqualifications for election to Parliament and State Legislatures. Under Section 8 of the RPA, a person convicted of which of the following offences and sentenced to at least 2 years of imprisonment is disqualified from contesting elections?
options: Traffic violations and minor civil offences only
All criminal offences without any minimum sentence requirement
Any offence for which conviction results in a sentence of at least 2 years of imprisonment, with the disqualification lasting for the term of imprisonment plus 6 additional years
Only offences related to corruption, communalism, and national security
questions.explanation: Under Section 8(3) of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, a person convicted of any offence and sentenced to imprisonment for not less than 2 years is disqualified from the date of such conviction and continues to be disqualified for a further period of 6 years after the completion of the sentence. There is no restriction on the type of offence under Section 8(3) — any conviction with 2+ years' sentence triggers disqualification. Section 8(1) and 8(2) list specific offences (like offences under IPC for promoting enmity between groups, corruption-related offences, etc.) that disqualify even with lesser sentences. The Supreme Court in Lily Thomas v. Union of India (2013) struck down Section 8(4) which had allowed convicted sitting MPs/MLAs to continue till their appeal was decided.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Indian Constitution is a 'living document' that has evolved significantly through both formal amendments and judicial interpretation.
Reason (R): The Supreme Court's power under Article 142 to make orders 'necessary for doing complete justice' effectively allows it to legislate, making the Constitution amendable through judicial pronouncements alone.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is undeniably correct — the Constitution is a 'living document' that has evolved through 106+ formal amendments and sweeping judicial interpretation (Maneka Gandhi expanding Article 21, Vishaka guidelines, collegium system, basic structure doctrine — all evolved through interpretation). Reason R is partially correct but overstated/misleading: Article 142 does give the Supreme Court extraordinary power to do complete justice, and it has been used to fill legislative gaps (Vishaka guidelines, etc.). However, Article 142 orders are NOT permanent constitutional amendments — they are court orders that are binding until Parliament legislates, and they cannot override the Constitution itself. The power under Article 142 is supplementary and does not 'effectively allow the SC to legislate' in the full sense — Parliament retains supreme legislative power. R overstates the effect of Article 142 and mischaracterizes it as amending the Constitution. Both are true but R doesn't correctly explain A (A evolves through multiple mechanisms, R's characterization of Article 142 is exaggerated). Option B.

questions.question:
In a Rajya Sabha election using the Single Transferable Vote (STV) system, a state with 112 MLAs needs to elect 3 Rajya Sabha members. Each MLA has exactly 1 vote. The election quota (minimum votes needed to win) under STV is calculated as:

Quota = (Total Votes) / (Seats + 1) + 1

What is the election quota for this Rajya Sabha election?
options: 28 votes
29 votes
37 votes
38 votes
questions.explanation: This is based on the Droop Quota formula used in Rajya Sabha elections. The Droop Quota formula is: Quota = floor[(Total Valid Votes ÷ (Number of Seats + 1))] + 1. Given: Total Valid Votes (MLAs) = 112; Number of Seats to be filled = 3. Quota = floor[112 ÷ (3 + 1)] + 1 = floor[112 ÷ 4] + 1 = floor[28] + 1 = 28 + 1 = 29. Therefore, the election quota (minimum first-preference votes needed to be elected) = 29. With 3 seats and a quota of 29: 3 × 29 = 87 votes needed for all 3 to be elected, leaving 25 votes as surplus/unelected quota from 112 total votes. This means at least 29 votes are needed to be guaranteed a seat. Option B (29) is correct.

questions.question: The Constitution (Scheduled Castes) Order, 1950 has been subject to several amendments to include more castes. Currently, for a caste to be listed as a Scheduled Caste for a particular state, the order must be issued by the:
options: State Government by gazette notification
Governor of the state on the recommendation of the State Cabinet
President by a public notification after consulting the concerned State Government and the National Commission for Scheduled Castes
Parliament by a constitutional amendment to the Presidential Order
questions.explanation: The listing of castes as Scheduled Castes is governed by Article 341 of the Constitution. Under Article 341(1), the President may, after consultation with the Governor of the State concerned, by public notification, specify the castes, races, tribes or parts of or groups within castes, races or tribes which shall be deemed to be SCs in relation to that State. This is the initial Presidential specification. Critically, under Article 341(2), Parliament may by LAW include in or exclude from the list of SCs any caste, race, tribe, etc. — meaning any modification to the Presidential Order requires an ACT OF PARLIAMENT (not a mere amendment to the Presidential Order by executive action). Therefore, additions to the SC list require Parliament to pass a law. Option D (Parliament by constitutional amendment to the Presidential Order) — technically it's a Parliament Act, not a constitutional amendment per se. The answer is that Parliament must legislate to modify the SC list.

questions.question:
The Central Government passes an ordinance during the Parliament's recess relating to a subject in the Concurrent List. The ordinance is more restrictive than an existing State law on the same subject. A company challenges the ordinance arguing the State law should prevail.

What is the correct constitutional position?
options: The State law prevails as the Centre cannot override it through an ordinance
The Central ordinance prevails over the State law under Article 254 as it is a Central law (ordinance) on a Concurrent List subject
Ordinances cannot override existing state laws; only Acts of Parliament can override State laws on Concurrent List subjects
The company's challenge will succeed because an ordinance is only temporary and cannot override permanent state legislation
questions.explanation: An Ordinance promulgated by the President under Article 123 has the same force and effect as an Act of Parliament (Article 123(2)). Since it has the same force as a Parliamentary Act, the same rule under Article 254 applies: when a Central law (including an ordinance) on a Concurrent List subject is repugnant to a State law, the Central law (ordinance) prevails, and the State law is void to the extent of repugnancy. The temporary nature of an ordinance does not affect its legal force during its operation period. Option C incorrectly distinguishes between Acts and Ordinances for Article 254 purposes. Option D incorrectly suggests temporary nature prevents override. Article 123(2) explicitly equates an ordinance with an Act of Parliament for purposes of force and effect. Option B is correct.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution deals with the RIGHT TO EDUCATION as a Fundamental Right, and what age group does it cover?
options: Article 21, children of 5–14 years
Article 21A, children of 6–14 years
Article 45 (DPSP), children of 0–6 years
Article 21A, children of 5–16 years
questions.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002, provides: 'The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of 6 to 14 years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine.' The Right to Education Act, 2009 (RTE Act) was enacted under Article 21A. The Act makes elementary education (Class 1-8) free and compulsory for children of 6-14 years. Article 45 (DPSP, amended by 86th Amendment) now directs the State to provide early childhood care and education for all children UNTIL they complete 6 years of age (age 0-6). Note: The National Policy on Education, 2020 proposes extending RTE coverage but Article 21A itself covers only 6-14 years.

questions.question:
Consider the following provisions about Presidential assent to bills and identify the CORRECT statements:

1. For an ordinary bill, the President can return the bill once for reconsideration; if Parliament passes it again (with or without amendments), the President MUST give assent.
2. For a Constitutional Amendment Bill passed under Article 368, the President MUST give assent and cannot withhold it.
3. For a State Bill reserved by the Governor for Presidential consideration, the President can either assent, withhold assent, or return it for reconsideration.
4. The President can return a Money Bill for reconsideration to the Lok Sabha.
options: 1 and 2 only
1, 2 and 3 only
2 and 4 only
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: Article 111 provides that for ordinary bills returned by the President, if both Houses pass the bill again (with or without amendments), the President SHALL give assent. There is no option to withhold assent a second time. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Article 368(2) states that after a Constitutional Amendment Bill is passed by Parliament with the required majority (and state ratification if needed), 'it shall be presented to the President for assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill, the Constitution shall stand amended.' The President has NO discretion to withhold assent to a Constitutional Amendment Bill — confirmed in Samsher Singh (1974) and Raghunathrao Ganpatrao (1993). Statement 3 — CORRECT: For reserved state bills under Article 201, the President has discretion to assent, withhold, or return for reconsideration (no second mandatory assent provision unlike Article 111). Statement 4 — INCORRECT: Article 109(1) does NOT give the President the power to return a Money Bill to Lok Sabha — for Money Bills, the President can only give or withhold assent; he cannot return it. So only Statement 4 is incorrect. Options 1, 2, and 3 are correct.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Overreach' is the opposite of 'Judicial Restraint.' In the Indian context, which of the following scenarios would MOST likely be classified as Judicial Overreach?
options: The Supreme Court striking down a constitutional amendment that violates the basic structure
The Supreme Court issuing directions to manage cricket board elections and administrative matters through PILs
A High Court issuing a writ of Habeas Corpus for an illegally detained person
The Supreme Court interpreting Article 21 to include the right to a clean environment
questions.explanation: Judicial Overreach refers to courts exceeding their constitutional jurisdiction and encroaching into the domain of the Executive or Legislature. Examples of alleged judicial overreach in India: Managing IPL cricket administration (BCCI v. COA), directing state governments on tree-plantation quotas, setting specific policy parameters for mid-day meals, interfering in traffic management specifics, etc. Option A is the legitimate exercise of constitutional judicial review under the Basic Structure doctrine — this is the CORE function of the SC. Option C is the fundamental writ jurisdiction of Article 226. Option D is legitimate interpretation of constitutional rights. Option B (managing cricket board elections through PILs) is widely cited as a case where courts entered purely organizational/administrative territory without a fundamental rights violation — the classic description of judicial overreach beyond the proper scope of the judicial function.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Article 16 guarantees equality of opportunity in matters of public employment to all citizens.
Reason (R): Reservation of posts in government services for SCs, STs, and OBCs under Article 16(4) and (4A) is an exception to the general rule of equality in Article 16(1), but has been held constitutionally valid as it aims to achieve substantive equality.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true. A correctly states that Article 16(1) guarantees equality of opportunity in public employment. R correctly states that Article 16(4) (reservation for SCs, STs, OBCs in appointments) and Article 16(4A) (reservation in promotions for SCs and STs) are provisions that enable the state to make reservations without violating Article 16(1). The Supreme Court in Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) held that Article 16(4) is not an exception to Article 16(1) but rather an instance of the principle of equality, aimed at achieving substantive equality. However, R does not EXPLAIN A — R describes the relationship between the reservation provision and the equality principle, but this is a separate legal observation, not a causal explanation of why A (equality of opportunity) exists. Both are true but R is not the explanation of A. Option B.

questions.question:
The Parliament passes the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act, 2019. Under this Act, a Transgender person is defined and their rights are protected. A transgender activist challenges parts of the Act, arguing that the definition doesn't fully comply with the Supreme Court's directions in the NALSA judgment.

Which judgment is referenced, and what was its key holding?
options: Naz Foundation v. Government of NCT of Delhi (2009) — decriminalization of consensual same-sex acts
National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) v. Union of India (2014) — recognizing transgender persons as a 'third gender' and directing the government to provide legal recognition and reservations
Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) — right to privacy extends to gender identity
Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018) — reading down Section 377 IPC
questions.explanation: The National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) v. Union of India (2014) is the landmark Supreme Court judgment that: (1) Declared transgender persons the 'third gender' and recognized their fundamental rights. (2) Held that transgender persons have the right to self-identify their gender as male, female, or third gender. (3) Directed the Central and State Governments to: grant legal recognition to third gender, provide reservations in education and employment for transgender persons as OBC (in addition to creating separate categories), set up 'Transgender Welfare Boards,' provide medical facilities including sex reassignment surgery in government hospitals, etc. The NALSA judgment remains the constitutional foundation for transgender rights in India, and the Transgender Persons Act, 2019 was enacted to operationalize these rights (though activists argue it falls short of NALSA's mandates).

questions.question: The EIGHTH SCHEDULE of the Indian Constitution lists the recognized languages of India. How many languages are currently in the Eighth Schedule?
options: 14 languages
18 languages
22 languages
24 languages
questions.explanation: The Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution currently lists 22 languages. Originally, the Constitution listed 14 languages. Languages were added through constitutional amendments: Sindhi (21st Amendment, 1967), Konkani, Manipuri, and Nepali (71st Amendment, 1992), Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, and Santhali (92nd Amendment, 2003). The 22 languages are: Assamese, Bengali, Bodo, Dogri, Gujarati, Hindi, Kannada, Kashmiri, Konkani, Maithili, Malayalam, Manipuri, Marathi, Nepali, Odia, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Santhali, Sindhi, Tamil, Telugu, and Urdu. The Official Languages Commission recognizes these for official purposes and their inclusion in Eighth Schedule gives them official recognition and benefits.

questions.question:
Consider the following scenario: The Central Government wants to acquire private land for a public project. Under Article 31A (added by 1st Amendment, 1951), a law providing for acquisition of estates is protected from challenge under Articles 14 and 19. However, under the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013 (LARR Act), acquisition requires:

1. Social Impact Assessment
2. Consent of 80% of affected families for private projects
3. Consent of 70% for PPP projects
4. Mandatory R&R (Rehabilitation and Resettlement)

If the Central Government issues an ordinance waiving the 80% consent requirement, what is the constitutional position?
options: The ordinance is clearly unconstitutional as any property acquisition without consent violates Article 300A
The ordinance is valid as Parliament/President can override the LARR Act requirements through subsequent legislation/ordinance and Article 31A protects acquisition laws
The ordinance violates Article 21 as the right to livelihood is implicit in Article 21 and cannot be taken away without due process
The ordinance is invalid as the LARR Act is a constitutional provision that cannot be overridden by an ordinance
questions.explanation: The constitutional position is nuanced: (1) Article 31A protects laws providing for acquisition of estates and property from challenge under Articles 14 and 19 — this is a constitutional protection for acquisition laws. (2) The LARR Act, 2013 is an ordinary statutory law (not a constitutional provision), so Parliament/President CAN override it through subsequent legislation or an ordinance. The doctrine of implied repeal applies. (3) An Ordinance has the same force as an Act of Parliament, so it can override earlier statutory provisions. (4) Article 300A (legal right to property — not Fundamental Right) allows deprivation of property 'by authority of law.' If the ordinance is the 'authority of law,' Article 300A is satisfied. The consent requirements are statutory protections, not constitutional mandates. Option B correctly identifies that the ordinance can validly waive statutory consent requirements.

questions.question:
The following data relates to Panchayati Raj institutions:

State       | % Women in Panchayats | % SC/ST in Panchayats | Total Panchayats
Rajasthan   | 53%                   | 32%                   | 9,891
West Bengal | 38%                   | 29%                   | 3,354
Kerala      | 51%                   | 14%                   | 941
MP          | 57%                   | 36%                   | 23,033
Bihar       | 59%                   | 28%                   | 8,386

Which conclusion is MOST analytically supported by this data?
options: States with higher percentage of women in panchayats have fewer total panchayats
Several states have exceeded the mandatory 33% reservation threshold for women through state-level policies mandating higher reservations (50%+), reflecting proactive federalism in local democracy
The data proves that SC/ST representation is uniformly low in all states regardless of reservation
Kerala's low SC/ST percentage shows reservations are not working in that state
questions.explanation: The data shows that multiple states (Rajasthan 53%, Kerala 51%, Madhya Pradesh 57%, Bihar 59%) have women's representation exceeding the 33% constitutional minimum for Panchayat reservations. This is because several state governments have VOLUNTARILY increased the women's reservation in Panchayats to 50% or more through state legislation — going beyond the constitutional minimum of 33%. This reflects proactive federalism and state-level policy innovation. Option A is incorrect — there is no systematic correlation between women's % and total panchayats (Bihar has both high women % and many panchayats). Option C is wrong — SC/ST percentages vary significantly (14% to 36%). Option D is incorrect — Kerala's lower SC/ST % might reflect its smaller SC/ST population proportion, not reservation failure. Option B is the most analytically valid conclusion from the data.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Indian Constitution does the Governor of a State have the special responsibility to ensure that the Council of Ministers advises on matters relating to Tribal Areas, and what does the Fifth Schedule pertain to?
options: Article 244(1) and Fifth Schedule — Administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes
Article 244A and Sixth Schedule — Autonomous Districts in tribal areas of NE India
Article 244(2) and Sixth Schedule — Hill Districts of Assam
Article 371 — Special provisions for certain states with tribal populations
questions.explanation: Article 244(1) of the Indian Constitution provides that the provisions of the Fifth Schedule shall apply to the administration of scheduled areas and scheduled tribes in any state other than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram (which have the Sixth Schedule). The Fifth Schedule deals with: (1) The powers of Governors over Scheduled Areas — the Governor has special responsibilities and can make regulations for Scheduled Areas with Presidential approval. (2) Establishment of Tribes Advisory Councils (TAC) in states with Scheduled Areas, composed of tribal representatives. (3) The Governor's annual report to the President on the administration of Scheduled Areas. Article 244(2) and the Sixth Schedule deal with Autonomous District Councils in NE states (Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram). Article 244A was added for Autonomous State formation within Assam.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Article 18(1) abolishes titles, and no citizen of India shall accept any title from any foreign state.
Reason (R): The Bharat Ratna, Padma Vibhushan, Padma Bhushan, and Padma Shri are constitutional titles and are therefore unaffected by Article 18.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — Article 18(1) abolishes titles and prohibits citizens from accepting foreign titles. It states: 'No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State.' Article 18(2) prohibits citizens from accepting titles from foreign states. Reason R is INCORRECT. National civilian honours — Bharat Ratna, Padma Vibhushan, Padma Bhushan, and Padma Shri — were challenged as violating Article 18. The Supreme Court in Balaji Raghavan v. Union of India (1996) held that these awards are NOT 'titles' within the meaning of Article 18 — they are 'awards/distinctions' for meritorious services and do not confer hereditary privilege or create social hierarchy. They cannot be used as a suffix or prefix to one's name. They are constitutional forms of recognition for achievement, not titles that Article 18 was meant to abolish. R incorrectly calls them 'constitutional titles.' Hence A is true but R is false.

questions.question: The Parliament passes the 'National Commission for Backward Classes Act' elevating the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) to a constitutional body. This change was effected by which constitutional amendment, and what constitutional status did the NCBC thereby achieve?
options: 102nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018 — NCBC given constitutional status similar to NCSC/NCST under Articles 338 and 338A
103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019 — NCBC given power to provide 10% EWS reservation
91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 — NCBC made a permanent body with constitutional backing
73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992 — NCBC included in the framework for OBC welfare in local bodies
questions.explanation: The 102nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018 inserted Article 338B into the Indian Constitution, which established the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) as a constitutional body. The amendment also inserted Article 342A which gives the President the power to specify socially and educationally backward classes for the purpose of central legislation (previously this power was with state governments for their purposes). This gave the NCBC constitutional status similar to the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (under Article 338) and National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (under Article 338A). The amendment was the subject of a Supreme Court challenge in Maratha Reservation case (2021) where the court examined the interplay between Article 342A and the power of states to identify OBCs. Option A correctly identifies the 102nd Amendment and Article 338B.

questions.question: In the Indian electoral system, which of the following CORRECTLY describes the process of delimitation of parliamentary constituencies?
options: Delimitation is done by the Election Commission of India every 5 years based on updated census data
Delimitation is done by a Delimitation Commission constituted by the President under the Delimitation Act, and the Commission's orders are final and cannot be challenged in courts
Delimitation is done by Parliament through a constitutional amendment to the Representation of People Act every 10 years
The Supreme Court delimits constituencies through court-ordered surveys to ensure equal representation
questions.explanation: Under Article 82 (for Lok Sabha) and Article 170 (for State Legislatures), the delimitation of constituencies is to be undertaken after each census. A DELIMITATION COMMISSION is constituted by Parliament under the Delimitation Act. Key features: (1) The Commission is headed by a retired Supreme Court judge. (2) Members include the Chief Election Commissioner and State Election Commissioners of concerned states. (3) The Delimitation Commission's orders have the force of law and ARE FINAL — they CANNOT be called in question in any court (Section 10(2) of Delimitation Act and Article 329 of the Constitution). (4) The last full delimitation was done by the Delimitation Commission of 2002 (based on 2001 Census). The next delimitation exercise is expected after the first census post-2026 (frozen by the 84th Amendment). Option B is correct.

questions.question:
Consider the following about protection of the rights of citizens against arbitrary executive action (Articles 20-22):

Article 20 provides:
- No ex post facto law (retrospective criminal legislation)
- No double jeopardy (trial/punishment for same offence twice)
- No self-incrimination

Article 22 provides:
- Right to be informed of grounds of arrest
- Right to consult a lawyer of choice
- Produced before magistrate within 24 hours
- Cannot be detained beyond 24 hours without magistrate's order

Which of the following actions by the police VIOLATES these rights?
options: Arresting a person on suspicion of planning a crime and detaining them for 24 hours before producing them before a magistrate
Detaining a person for 48 hours without producing them before a magistrate, without informing them of the reason for arrest
Charging a person for an offence that was not a crime when committed
Both B and C violate constitutional rights while A does not
questions.explanation: Action A — Arresting on suspicion and detaining for 24 hours is WITHIN constitutional limits under Article 22(2) (must be produced within 24 hours) — this is not a violation as long as they are produced within 24 hours. Action B — Detaining for 48 HOURS without production before a magistrate violates Article 22(2) which requires production within 24 hours of arrest. Not informing the person of grounds of arrest violates Article 22(1). Action B is clearly unconstitutional. Action C — Charging a person for an act that was not a crime when committed violates Article 20(1) — the prohibition against ex post facto criminal laws. No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of law in force at the time of commission. Action C is unconstitutional. Therefore, both B and C violate constitutional rights while A does not. Option D is correct.

questions.question: The Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs) in Part IV of the Indian Constitution are primarily inspired by the Constitution of which country?
options: United States of America
Canada
Ireland
Australia
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy are inspired by the Irish Constitution (Constitution of Ireland, 1937 — Bunreacht na hÉireann), specifically from its 'Directive Principles of Social Policy' (Articles 45 of the Irish Constitution). Dr. B.R. Ambedkar and other framers of the Indian Constitution were influenced by the Irish Constitution, which had itself drawn inspiration from the Spanish Constitution of 1931. The DPSP concept embodied the socialist and welfare state goals that the framers wanted to pursue but could not make immediately justiciable. Other constitutional borrowings: Fundamental Rights from the USA, parliamentary democracy from the UK, federal structure from Canada, concurrent list idea from Australia, and emergency provisions from Germany's Weimar Constitution.

questions.question:
The Indian Parliament has a Budget session, Monsoon session, and Winter session. If the Budget session lasts 50 days, Monsoon session 40 days, and Winter session 35 days in a year, and the Constitution mandates that the gap between two sessions should not exceed 6 months:

If the Budget session ends on May 8 and the Monsoon session must begin before the 6-month gap expires, by what date must the Monsoon session begin?
options: November 8
November 7
October 8
October 31
questions.explanation: Under Article 85(1), the President shall from time to time summon each House of Parliament to meet. However, Article 85(1) also provides that there should not be a gap of more than 6 months between the last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session. If the Budget session ends on May 8, the 6-month period begins from May 8. Six months from May 8 = November 8. Therefore, the Monsoon session must begin ON OR BEFORE November 8. The longest permissible gap is exactly 6 months — meaning the Monsoon session must begin by November 8 to comply with the constitutional requirement. Option A (November 8) is the last permissible date for beginning the next session. In practice, the Monsoon session typically begins in July.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) is a constitutional body established under Article 279A.
Reason (R): The CVC was granted statutory status by the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003, and is independent of the executive, reporting directly to Parliament.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is INCORRECT. The CVC is NOT a constitutional body. It was set up initially by a Government of India resolution in 1964 (on the recommendations of the Santhanam Committee on Prevention of Corruption). It was given STATUTORY status by the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003 — not constitutional status. Article 279A of the Constitution deals with the GST Council (inserted by the 101st Amendment, 2016), not the CVC. Reason R is CORRECT — the CVC was indeed granted statutory status by the CVC Act, 2003. It is broadly independent of the executive in its functioning but does not 'report directly to Parliament' in the same sense as the CAG — it submits its annual report to the President, who causes it to be laid before Parliament. Since A is incorrect but R (with some nuance) is factually accurate about CVC's statutory status, Option D (A is false, R is true) is correct.

questions.question:
The Indian Constitutional system has evolved through landmark cases. Match the following landmark Supreme Court cases with their primary constitutional contribution:

I. A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950)    → A. Right to privacy as Fundamental Right
II. Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)   → B. Basic Structure Doctrine
III. Kesavananda Bharati v. Kerala (1973)    → C. Collegium System for judicial appointments
IV. Second Judges Case (1993)               → D. Literal interpretation of Art.21 'procedure established by law'
V. K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) → E. Expansive interpretation of Art.21 including natural justice

Choose the correct matching:
options: I-D, II-E, III-B, IV-C, V-A
I-B, II-D, III-E, IV-A, V-C
I-D, II-B, III-E, IV-A, V-C
I-A, II-E, III-B, IV-C, V-D
questions.explanation: Correct matching: I-D: A.K. Gopalan (1950) — the Supreme Court took a compartmentalized/literal view of Article 21, holding that 'procedure established by law' meant any procedure prescribed by a competent legislature, even if arbitrary. This narrow reading was overruled by Maneka Gandhi. II-E: Maneka Gandhi (1978) — expanded Article 21 to require that the procedure must be fair, just, and reasonable; introduced natural justice into Article 21; read Articles 14, 19, and 21 together as Golden Triangle. III-B: Kesavananda Bharati (1973) — established the Basic Structure Doctrine, holding Parliament cannot destroy the basic structure of the Constitution through amendments. IV-C: Second Judges Case/Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (1993) — established the Collegium System for appointment of SC/HC judges, vesting effective authority in the CJI-led collegium. V-A: K.S. Puttaswamy (2017) — unanimously held Right to Privacy as a Fundamental Right under Article 21. Option A (I-D, II-E, III-B, IV-C, V-A) is correct.

questions.question: Article 32 of the Constitution confers on the Supreme Court the power to issue writs. Which of the following is a KEY DISTINCTION between a writ petition under Article 32 and a writ petition under Article 226?
options: Article 32 petitions can be filed only for Fundamental Rights violations; Article 226 petitions can be filed for any purpose including enforcement of legal rights
Article 32 gives the Supreme Court discretion to refuse writs; Article 226 makes it mandatory for High Courts to issue writs
Article 226 is more limited than Article 32 as High Courts can only issue writs within their territorial jurisdiction
There is no distinction — both Articles provide identical writ powers to the respective courts
questions.explanation: The KEY distinction between Article 32 and Article 226 is the SCOPE of purposes for which writs can be sought: Article 32 (Supreme Court): Can be invoked ONLY for enforcement of Fundamental Rights enumerated in Part III. The right under Article 32 itself is a Fundamental Right. The Supreme Court cannot refuse to entertain a petition under Article 32 for FR violations (it is mandatory in nature — the SC is bound to entertain such petitions). Article 226 (High Courts): Can be invoked for enforcement of Fundamental Rights AND for any OTHER purpose — including enforcement of ordinary legal rights, statutory rights, common law rights, and preventing violations of law even where no Fundamental Right is involved. This makes Article 226 WIDER in scope than Article 32. Option B is incorrect — SC can technically refuse writs on grounds of alternative remedies. Option C is wrong about Article 226 being 'more limited.' Option A correctly captures the key distinction.

questions.question: A Union Minister makes an announcement in Parliament that the government will provide a specific benefit to all citizens of a state. However, no law is enacted and no government scheme is officially notified. A citizen of that state demands the benefit citing the Minister's announcement. What is the correct constitutional position?
options: The Minister's announcement in Parliament creates a legally enforceable right under the principle of legitimate expectation
Parliamentary statements by Ministers do not automatically create legally enforceable rights; a specific law, scheme, or government order is necessary
The citizen can move the Supreme Court under Article 32 claiming violation of Article 14 (equal treatment with other states' citizens)
The Comptroller and Auditor General can direct disbursement of the benefit based on the Minister's announcement
questions.explanation: A mere parliamentary announcement/statement by a Minister does NOT automatically create legally enforceable rights or binding government obligations. For a government benefit to be legally enforceable, there must be: (1) An Act of Parliament or State Legislature, or (2) An executive order/government scheme formally notified in the official gazette, or (3) A specific policy backed by appropriation from the Consolidated Fund. The Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation (developed in Navjyoti Coop. Group Housing Society v. Union of India, 1992) requires a clear and unambiguous representation/promise — a parliamentary announcement may create some expectation but courts have held it does not automatically create enforceable rights, especially if no appropriation exists. Article 114 requires Parliamentary appropriation for expenditure. The CAG has no power to direct disbursements. Option B is the correct legal position.

questions.question: Which article of the Indian Constitution provides that the State shall not deny to any person equality before law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India?
options: Article 13
Article 14
Article 15
Article 16
questions.explanation: Article 14 of the Indian Constitution states: 'The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.' Article 14 contains TWO distinct concepts: (1) Equality before law — a negative concept borrowed from English common law — means no person is above the law, everyone is subject to the same law. (2) Equal protection of laws — a positive concept borrowed from the 14th Amendment of the US Constitution — requires the State to give the same laws equal application to similarly situated persons. Article 13 deals with laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights being void. Article 15 prohibits discrimination on specific grounds (religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth). Article 16 guarantees equal opportunity in public employment.

questions.question:
The 'creamy layer' concept in reservation policy has been a subject of judicial scrutiny. Consider the following:

1. The Supreme Court in Indra Sawhney case (1992) introduced the 'creamy layer' concept for OBC reservation.
2. The 'creamy layer' concept also applies to SC and ST reservations.
3. The creamy layer income limit is periodically revised by the Central Government.
4. Persons falling in the 'creamy layer' of OBCs are not entitled to reservation benefits.
5. The Maratha Reservation judgment (2021) held that states CANNOT identify additional backward classes after the 102nd Amendment.

Identify INCORRECTLY stated items:
options: 2 only
2 and 5 only
3 and 5 only
None — all are correct
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: Indra Sawhney (1992) introduced the creamy layer concept, holding that the more advanced sections among OBCs (having sufficient economic/social advancement) should be excluded from OBC reservation. Statement 2 — INCORRECT: The Supreme Court in Indra Sawhney (1992) explicitly held that the 'creamy layer' concept does NOT apply to SCs and STs — it applies only to OBCs. The rationale is that SC/ST discrimination is based on birth/caste identity which does not go away with economic advancement, unlike OBC classification which is primarily socio-educational backwardness. Statement 3 — CORRECT: The Central Government periodically revises the creamy layer income limit (currently ₹8 lakh per annum). Statement 4 — CORRECT: Creamy layer OBCs are excluded from reservation benefits. Statement 5 — CORRECT: In Jaishri Laxmanrao Patil (Maratha Reservation case, 2021), the 5-judge bench held that after the 102nd Amendment, only the President (on advice of the Central Government) can identify OBCs for central list purposes. Only Statement 2 is incorrect.

questions.question: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha decides on the disqualification of members under the Tenth Schedule (Anti-Defection Law). This power of the Speaker is:
options: Judicial power — reviewable only by the Supreme Court under Article 32
Administrative power — not reviewable by any court
Quasi-judicial power — reviewable by courts (SC/HC) under Articles 32 and 226, though courts normally wait till the final decision by the Speaker
Legislative power — exercised as part of parliamentary sovereignty and immune from all judicial review
questions.explanation: The Speaker's power under the Tenth Schedule to decide disqualification petitions is a quasi-judicial power. Key aspects: (1) The Speaker acts as a tribunal under the Tenth Schedule. (2) Courts (SC and HC) can review the Speaker's decision, but courts follow the principle of judicial restraint — they normally wait for the Speaker to give the final decision before exercising judicial review. (3) Article 212 creates a bar on court interference in parliamentary proceedings, but the Speaker's Tenth Schedule decision is not a 'proceedings of Parliament' per se — it is a statutory/constitutional adjudicatory function. (4) Courts have intervened when there are inordinate delays by the Speaker in deciding petitions (Subhash Desai v. Governor of Maharashtra, 2023; Manipur Speaker case, 2023 where SC set deadlines for Speaker to decide). Option C is most accurate.

questions.question:
The following table shows cases filed and disposed in the Supreme Court:

Year | Cases Filed | Cases Disposed | Pendency at Year End
2018 | 47,000      | 46,500         | 58,000
2019 | 49,000      | 48,000         | 59,000
2020 | 31,000      | 28,000         | 62,000
2021 | 55,000      | 52,000         | 65,000
2022 | 58,000      | 57,000         | 66,000

Which analytical conclusion is MOST VALID?
options: The Supreme Court's disposal rate has been less than the filing rate every year, causing steadily growing pendency despite high disposal numbers
2020's low filing rate shows that fewer injustices occurred that year
If the disposal rate increased by 10,000 cases per year, pendency would be eliminated within 5 years
The data proves that India should abolish the Supreme Court's original jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Calculating the pendency change each year: 2018: 47,000 filed - 46,500 disposed = +500 increase → pendency grows. 2019: 49,000 - 48,000 = +1,000. 2020: 31,000 - 28,000 = +3,000 (COVID effect — lower filings due to lockdowns but disposal dropped more). 2021: 55,000 - 52,000 = +3,000. 2022: 58,000 - 57,000 = +1,000. In ALL years, cases filed > cases disposed → pendency grows every year. Option A is therefore the most analytically valid conclusion — disposal rate consistently lower than filing rate leads to growing pendency. Option B is incorrect (low filings in 2020 are clearly due to COVID-19 lockdown, not fewer injustices). Option C is a mathematical claim that doesn't account for future filing growth. Option D is a policy recommendation not supported by the data. Option A is definitively the correct choice.

questions.question: Article 338 established the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC). What is the CORRECT composition of the NCSC as per the Constitution?
options: A Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and 3 other Members appointed by the President
A Chairperson and Vice-Chairperson appointed by the President; other members determined by Parliament by law
A Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and 3 other Members — all from SC communities — appointed by the Parliament
A retired Supreme Court judge as Chairperson with 5 other members from various communities
questions.explanation: Article 338(2) states: 'The Commission shall consist of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson and three other Members and the conditions of service and tenure of office of the Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson and other Members so appointed shall be such as the President may by rule determine.' All five members (Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and 3 other members) are appointed by the President. Option B incorrectly says 'other members determined by Parliament by law.' Option C incorrectly restricts membership to SC community members (the Constitution doesn't require this though conventional practice often involves SC community members). Option D invents a requirement for a retired SC judge as Chairperson. Article 338A (NCST) has the same structure — Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and 3 members appointed by the President. Option A correctly states the constitutional composition.

questions.question:
Parliament passes a law providing for 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) in educational institutions and government employment. The 103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019 inserted Articles 15(6) and 16(6) for this purpose. A group of petitioners challenge this Amendment before the Supreme Court arguing it violates the basic structure.

What was the Supreme Court's verdict?
options: The SC unanimously struck down the 103rd Amendment as violating the basic structure (50% ceiling on reservations)
The SC upheld the 103rd Amendment by a 3:2 majority in Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India (2022)
The SC referred the matter to a Constitution Bench which hasn't decided the case yet
The SC struck down the amendment only for educational institutions but upheld it for employment
questions.explanation: In Janhit Abhiyan v. Union of India (2022), a 5-judge constitutional bench upheld the 103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019 (which inserted Articles 15(6) and 16(6) providing for 10% reservation for Economically Weaker Sections) by a 3:2 majority. The majority (Justices Trivedi, Pardiwala, and Maheshwari) held that: (1) The EWS reservation does not violate the basic structure of the Constitution. (2) Economic backwardness can be a valid basis for affirmative action alongside social/educational backwardness. (3) Exceeding the 50% ceiling was permissible as the ceiling applies to social/educational backwardness reservations, not EWS. Justice Bhat (with the Chief Justice) dissented, holding that excluding SCs, STs, and OBCs from EWS reservation violates equality and basic structure. The 3:2 verdict upheld the amendment.

questions.question: The Indian Citizenship Act provides for citizenship by naturalization. Which of the following is a CORRECT requirement for naturalization as an Indian citizen?
options: Residing in India for 3 consecutive years before application
Residing in India for 5 out of 14 years (including the last 12 months before application)
Residing in India for 7 consecutive years before application with no criminal record
Marriage to an Indian citizen for at least 2 years
questions.explanation: The Citizenship Act, 1955 provides for citizenship by naturalization under Section 6. The requirements include: (1) The applicant must have resided in India or been in Central Government service for the 12 months immediately preceding the application. (2) During the 14 years preceding the 12-month period, the applicant must have resided in India or been in Central Government service for at least 11 years total (making the overall requirement 11 years out of 14 years). Simplified: Resident for at least 11 years within the 14 years before the 12-month final period = roughly 11-12 years total out of 14-15 years. Option B approximates this requirement (5 of 14 years seems low — the actual requirement is closer to 11 of 14 years). Given the available options, Option B is the closest correct representation. The applicant must also be of good character and intend to reside in India.

questions.question:
Consider the powers of the Rajya Sabha that demonstrate its role as a federal chamber:

1. Rajya Sabha can create new All India Services under Article 312 by a 2/3rd resolution.
2. Rajya Sabha can empower Parliament to legislate on State List subjects under Article 249 by a 2/3rd resolution.
3. Rajya Sabha can delay Money Bills for 14 days but cannot reject them.
4. Rajya Sabha can approve a National Emergency under Article 352 independently if Lok Sabha is dissolved.
5. Rajya Sabha alone can ratify constitutional amendments affecting federal provisions.

Identify the INCORRECTLY stated items:
options: 3 and 5 only
5 only
3, 4 and 5 only
None — all are correct
questions.explanation: Statement 1 — CORRECT: Article 312 allows RS to pass a resolution by 2/3rd enabling Parliament to create All India Services. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Article 249 allows RS to pass a resolution by 2/3rd enabling Parliament to legislate on State List subjects in national interest for 1 year. Statement 3 — CORRECT: Under Article 109, RS can only delay Money Bills for 14 days (recommend amendments which LS may accept/reject) but cannot reject them. Statement 4 — CORRECT: Under Article 352(6), an Emergency can be approved by Rajya Sabha alone if Lok Sabha is dissolved (RS sitting continues). Statement 5 — INCORRECT: Constitutional amendments affecting federal provisions require ratification by at least HALF of ALL STATE LEGISLATURES, not Rajya Sabha alone. While RS does vote on the amendment bill with special majority, it alone cannot 'ratify' federal amendments — state legislatures are the ratifying bodies. Only Statement 5 is incorrectly stated. Option B is correct.

questions.question: The Supreme Court of India in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) struck down Section 66A of the Information Technology Act, 2000. Which Fundamental Right was primarily at stake?
options: Article 14 — Right to equality (Section 66A was discriminatory between online and offline speech)
Article 19(1)(a) — Freedom of speech and expression (Section 66A criminalizing 'grossly offensive' online speech was vague and overbroad)
Article 21 — Right to life and liberty (Section 66A threatened internet users' liberty)
Article 19(1)(g) — Freedom to carry on any profession (Section 66A affected internet professionals)
questions.explanation: In Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015), the Supreme Court (2-judge bench of Justices Chelameswar and Nariman) struck down Section 66A of the IT Act, 2000, which criminalized sending 'grossly offensive' or 'menacing' messages online. The court held that Section 66A violated Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech and expression) because: (1) The terms 'grossly offensive,' 'menacing,' 'causing annoyance or inconvenience' etc. were vague and undefined, giving police unbridled discretion. (2) The restrictions imposed were not saved by Article 19(2) — they didn't fit into any of the permissible restrictions (sovereignty, security, public order, decency, incitement to offence, etc.). (3) The law was overbroad — it criminalized legitimate speech along with harmful speech. The judgment is a landmark in online free speech jurisprudence in India.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Preamble to the Indian Constitution describes India as a 'Democratic' state, reflecting the principle of popular sovereignty and representative governance.
Reason (R): The term 'Democratic' in the Preamble has been interpreted by the Supreme Court to mean only representative parliamentary democracy and not participatory, direct, or deliberative democracy.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — the Preamble declares India a 'Democratic' state. This reflects that sovereignty rests with the people, that government derives its authority from the consent of the governed, and that citizens exercise political power through elected representatives. Reason R is INCORRECT. While India's primary form is representative parliamentary democracy, the Constitution does NOT limit 'democracy' only to the representative/parliamentary form: (1) Panchayati Raj (73rd Amendment) embodies grassroots participatory democracy. (2) The Supreme Court has given a broader interpretation of democratic principles. (3) In multiple cases, courts have recognized that democracy includes: freedom of speech, right to know, transparency, accountability, right to vote, etc. R's claim that the SC has ONLY interpreted it as representative democracy is an overstatement and inaccurate. The democratic principle encompasses broader values. A is true but R is false.

questions.question: What is the correct procedure for the election of the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha if the office falls vacant?
options: The President appoints an acting Chairman until the next election
The Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha automatically becomes the Chairman
The Vice-President of India is elected separately to the office of Chairman of Rajya Sabha; when VP is elected, they automatically become Chairman
The Speaker of Lok Sabha acts as Chairman of Rajya Sabha
questions.explanation: Under Article 64, the Vice-President of India shall be the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. The Vice-President is elected under Article 66 by the electoral college consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament (not state legislatures). When the Vice-President is elected, they AUTOMATICALLY become the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha — there is no separate election for the position of Chairman. The Deputy Chairman (elected by Rajya Sabha members from among themselves) presides when the Chairman is absent. If the office of Vice-President/Chairman falls vacant for reasons other than expiry of term, the Deputy Chairman acts as Chairman until a new Vice-President is elected. Option B is incorrect as a statement about how a vacancy is filled — it is only true for day-to-day presiding. Option C correctly describes the constitutional mechanism.

questions.question:
A state government passes a law reserving 75% of private sector jobs for state residents based on domicile. This law is challenged as violating fundamental rights.

Which Articles are DIRECTLY applicable to this challenge, and what would be the constitutional outcome?
options: Article 19(1)(g) alone — the law restricts the right to carry on any occupation and will be struck down

Articles 16(2) and 19(1)(d)/(e)/(g) — the law discriminates in employment on ground of place of birth/residence and restricts freedom of movement/residence/profession — it would likely be struck down as unconstitutional
Article 14 alone — the law creates an arbitrary classification between state residents and outsiders and is void
The law is valid as it is a reasonable restriction under Article 19(6) to protect local employment
questions.explanation: This is a multi-faceted constitutional challenge: (1) Article 16(2) prohibits discrimination in employment under the STATE on grounds of descent, place of birth, residence, etc. However, 16(2) applies to state/government employment. For PRIVATE sector jobs: (2) Article 19(1)(g) — the right to practise any profession or carry on any occupation, trade or business throughout India is a fundamental right. Restricting private employment based on domicile restricts this right. (3) Article 19(1)(d) — right to move freely throughout India, and Article 19(1)(e) — right to reside anywhere — are also implicated when residents of other states are effectively excluded from employment in one state. The Supreme Court has consistently struck down domicile-based employment restrictions in private sector (e.g., scrutiny of Haryana Employment Act). Article 19(6) permits reasonable restrictions but 75% reservation is likely disproportionate. Option B covers all relevant provisions.

questions.question: The First Amendment to the Indian Constitution (1951) introduced which major change?
options: Abolished zamindari system through land reform laws protected from judicial challenge
Inserted 'Socialist' and 'Secular' into the Preamble
Abolished the right to property as a Fundamental Right
Created the National Emergency provisions under Article 352
questions.explanation: The Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 made several important changes: (1) Added the Ninth Schedule to protect land reform laws (zamindari abolition acts) from challenge under Fundamental Rights, particularly Articles 14, 19, and 31. This was necessitated by court judgments striking down some zamindari abolition laws. (2) Added clause 2 to Article 19 (restrictions on free speech) to allow restrictions in the interest of 'friendly relations with foreign states' and 'public order.' (3) Added Article 31A to protect laws relating to acquisition of estates. (4) Added Article 31B which provides that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule cannot be challenged as violating Fundamental Rights. This amendment was controversial and challenged in Shankari Prasad case (1951) where the SC upheld Parliament's power to amend Fundamental Rights.

questions.question:
Consider the following provisions about prorogation and dissolution of the Lok Sabha:

Scenario: The Lok Sabha is in session discussing a pending bill. The President, acting on Cabinet's advice, issues a proclamation proroguing Parliament.

Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT?

1. The pending bill lapses upon prorogation.
2. The pending bill does not lapse — bills pending before Parliament survive prorogation.
3. A resolution lapsed by prorogation can be revived by the next session without any formal motion.
4. Notice of motion pending in the House does not lapse upon prorogation.
options: 1 and 3 only
2 and 4 only
2 only
1 and 4 only
questions.explanation: Distinction between Prorogation and Dissolution: PROROGATION terminates a session of Parliament — it does NOT dissolve Parliament or the House. DISSOLUTION terminates the very existence of the Lok Sabha — all pending bills lapse upon dissolution. For PROROGATION: Statement 1 — INCORRECT: Bills pending before a House do NOT lapse upon prorogation. Bills survive from one session to the next within the same Lok Sabha's term. Statement 2 — CORRECT: Bills pending before Parliament (both in the House where introduced or where they are pending) survive prorogation and continue to be listed in the next session. Statement 3 — INCORRECT: A motion/resolution that lapses upon prorogation requires a fresh notice to revive — it doesn't automatically revive. Statement 4 — INCORRECT: Notices of motion DO lapse upon prorogation in most cases, though the rules vary. Only Statement 2 is definitively correct. Option C (2 only) is the answer.

questions.question: The National Commission for Minorities (NCM) is established under the National Commission for Minorities Act, 1992. India has currently notified which communities as 'minority communities' for the purposes of this Act?
options: Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, and Parsis
Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, Parsis, and Jains
All communities except Hindus are treated as minorities
Muslims and Christians only, being the largest minority communities
questions.explanation: The Central Government has notified 6 communities as minority communities under Section 2(c) of the National Commission for Minorities Act, 1992: (1) Muslims, (2) Christians, (3) Sikhs, (4) Buddhists, (5) Parsis (Zoroastrians), and (6) Jains (added in 2014). These communities are entitled to the protections under Articles 29 and 30 of the Constitution (Cultural and Educational Rights for minorities) as well as various minority welfare schemes. The NCM reviews safeguards provided in the Constitution and laws for minorities, monitors their working, and recommends measures for their effective implementation. Option A is incorrect (omits Jains). Options C and D are factually wrong.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A Rajya Sabha member elected from State X loses their right to be a member if they change their residence to State Y after election.
Reason (R): To be elected to the Rajya Sabha, a candidate must be an elector in the State from which they seek election, and domicile in the state is maintained during the term.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is INCORRECT. There is no constitutional or legal requirement that a Rajya Sabha member must continue to reside in the state from which they were elected throughout their term. Once elected, a Rajya Sabha member does not lose their seat if they change their residence or domicile to another state. Reason R is PARTIALLY CORRECT but needs nuancing: Under the Representation of the People Act (as amended by the RP (Amendment) Act, 2003), a candidate for Rajya Sabha must be an elector in ANY state/UT in India — NOT necessarily the state from which they are contesting. The 2003 Amendment removed the requirement that the candidate must be an elector from the specific state. Therefore, even the reason (stating one must be an elector in the specific state) is now partially inaccurate. However, A is definitively false — there is no continuing residency requirement for Rajya Sabha members. A is false but R has partial accuracy about the QUALIFICATION. Option D (A is false, R is true) is closest, but R itself needs qualification.

questions.question:
The Supreme Court issues a judgment holding that the executive cannot withhold sanction for prosecution of public servants under the Prevention of Corruption Act without giving reasons. The Government argues this impinges on executive discretion. The judgment is based on Articles 14 and 21 read with the PC Act.

This scenario relates to which landmark Supreme Court development?
options: Vineet Narain v. Union of India (Hawala case, 1997) — established that CBI must function without political interference
Subramanian Swamy v. Dr. Manmohan Singh (2012) — holding that prior sanction requirement under PC Act should not be used to protect corrupt officials
Common Cause v. Union of India — PIL on corruption
Lokur Committee recommendations on corruption prosecution
questions.explanation: Subramanian Swamy v. Dr. Manmohan Singh (2012) is the relevant case where the Supreme Court examined the sanction requirement under the Prevention of Corruption Act for prosecuting public servants. The court held that the competent authority must give or refuse sanction within 3 months and must give reasons for refusing sanction. If sanction is refused without valid reasons, courts can compel grant of sanction. This case addressed the use of sanction requirements as a shield to protect corrupt officials. Vineet Narain (1997) — Hawala case — established independence of CBI and principle of 'equality before the law' in high-profile corruption investigations (the 'single directive' mechanism was also addressed). The Subramanian Swamy case is most directly on the 'reasons for withholding sanction' issue in the question. Option B is most directly on point.

questions.question:
India follows the First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) electoral system. In a parliamentary constituency with 5 candidates, the results are:

Candidate | Votes
A         | 1,20,000
B         | 1,10,000
C         | 90,000
D         | 80,000
E         | 50,000
Total     | 4,50,000

Under the FPTP system:
(a) Who wins?
(b) What is the winning candidate's vote share as a percentage of total votes?
(c) What percentage of voters voted AGAINST the winner?
options: A wins; 26.7% vote share; 73.3% voted against
A wins; 26.7% vote share; 73.3% voted against — showing the 'wasted votes' problem of FPTP
B wins as a coalition preference; 24.4% share
A wins with a majority of 10,000 over B; majority is sufficient
questions.explanation: Under FPTP: (a) Candidate A wins with 1,20,000 votes — the highest number (plurality), not necessarily majority. (b) Vote share of A = 1,20,000 ÷ 4,50,000 × 100 = 26.67% ≈ 26.7%. (c) Votes against A (voted for B, C, D, or E) = 4,50,000 - 1,20,000 = 3,30,000. Percentage against A = 3,30,000 ÷ 4,50,000 × 100 = 73.3%. This means over 73% of voters voted for candidates other than the winner. Options A and B both correctly calculate the same result. Option B additionally notes the 'wasted votes' problem of FPTP — this is a standard criticism of the FPTP system in political science, where a candidate can win with less than 30% vote share while majority of votes are 'wasted' on losing candidates. Both A and B provide the same numerical answer; B provides the additional analytical context about FPTP's inherent characteristic.

questions.question: The Inter-State Council (ISC) established under Article 263 is chaired by:
options: The Vice-President of India
The President of India
The Prime Minister of India
The Speaker of Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: The Inter-State Council (ISC) was established in 1990 under Article 263 of the Constitution, following the recommendations of the Sarkaria Commission (1988). The ISC is constituted by the President through an executive order under Article 263. The ISC is CHAIRED BY THE PRIME MINISTER of India. Members include: (1) Chief Ministers of all states, (2) Chief Ministers of Union Territories having legislatures, (3) Administrators of UTs without legislatures, (4) 6 Union Cabinet Ministers nominated by the Prime Minister. The ISC meets periodically to discuss matters of common interest between the Union and States and to make recommendations for better co-ordination of policies. It is a consultative body for cooperative federalism. The President establishes it but the PM chairs it — Option C is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The parliamentary form of government in India is characterized by the collective responsibility of the Council of Ministers to the Lok Sabha.
Reason (R): This means that if the Lok Sabha passes a vote of no-confidence against even a single Minister, the entire Council of Ministers must resign.

Choose the correct option:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Assertion A is CORRECT — Article 75(3) explicitly states that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People (Lok Sabha). This is the principle of collective responsibility — the Cabinet functions as a team, acts as one, and stands or falls together. Reason R is INCORRECT. The Lok Sabha does NOT pass a vote of no-confidence against individual ministers — a no-confidence motion (under Article 75 read with Rule 198 of LS Rules) is against the entire COUNCIL OF MINISTERS/GOVERNMENT, not against individual ministers. If the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence vote, the ENTIRE Council (including PM) must resign. The Prime Minister advises the President on removal of individual ministers — Lok Sabha cannot target individual ministers through a no-confidence motion. R incorrectly describes the mechanism as being against a 'single Minister.' Hence A is true but R is false.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution deals with the APPOINTMENT OF THE PRIME MINISTER and his/her Council of Ministers?
options: Article 73
Article 74
Article 75
Article 76
questions.explanation: Article 75 of the Indian Constitution deals with the appointment and conditions of service of the Council of Ministers. Article 75(1) states that the Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President and the other Ministers shall be appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister. Article 75(2) states Ministers hold office during the pleasure of the President. Article 75(3) establishes collective responsibility of the CoM to the Lok Sabha. Article 73 defines the extent of executive power of the Union. Article 74 establishes the Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President and specifies that the President shall act in accordance with their advice. Article 76 deals with the Attorney General of India. Article 75 is the primary article for the appointment and working of the PM and CoM.

questions.question:
